questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "Without food for a week, most of the birds found other places to eat." That is why fewer returned.

questions.prompt: Ben told everyone he wasn't scared of the tall slide. But at the top, he held the railing tight and didn't move. "Go ahead!" called his friend. Ben looked down, then said, "I think I'll let you go first." Which sentence best shows that Ben was really scared, even though he said he wasn't?
choices: He held the railing tight and didn't move
He told everyone he wasn't scared
questions.correct: He held the railing tight and didn't move
questions.hint: Find the action that his body does, not the words he says. Actions can show a feeling words are hiding.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Even though Ben said he wasn't scared, gripping the rail and freezing shows his body was really afraid.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! His WORDS say he is not scared, but his body tells the truth. Which action shows he was really afraid?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "He held the railing tight and didn't move." His body showed fear even though he said he wasn't scared.

questions.prompt: Rosa planted a bean seed and checked it every single day. For a week, nothing happened, and she frowned each time. On Monday she almost forgot to look. When she did, a tiny green sprout had pushed through the dirt. Rosa clapped her hands. How did Rosa's feelings change from the beginning to the end of the story?
choices: From impatient and disappointed to happy and surprised
questions.correct: From impatient and disappointed to happy and surprised
questions.hint: Notice how she felt while she waited, and how she felt the moment something finally changed. Track both feelings.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Rosa frowned and nearly gave up while waiting, then clapped when the sprout came. Her feelings really changed.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Look at the start, when nothing grew, and the end, when the sprout appeared. How did her feelings flip?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "From impatient and disappointed to happy and surprised." She frowned while waiting, then clapped with joy.

questions.prompt: The two goats stood on a narrow log over the river. Neither would step back to let the other cross. They pushed and pushed until SPLASH — both goats fell into the cold water. What lesson does this story most likely teach?
choices: If nobody is willing to give way, everyone can lose
It is fun to push your friends
questions.correct: If nobody is willing to give way, everyone can lose
questions.hint: Think about WHY both goats ended up wet. What could they have done differently? The lesson is about their choice.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Neither goat would step back, so they both fell in. The lesson is that if no one gives way, everyone can lose.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Think about WHY both goats ended up in the water. Neither would step back. What lesson does that teach?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "If nobody is willing to give way, everyone can lose." Neither goat stepped back, so both fell in.

standards.standard_description: Ask and answer who, what, where, when, why, how questions about key details in a text.
standards.parent_tip: When reading nonfiction, pause and ask your child specific questions about who, what, where, when, and why.

questions.prompt:
Honeybees live in hives. A hive can have up to 60,000 bees! The queen bee lays all the eggs, and worker bees collect nectar from flowers.

What does the queen bee do?
questions.hint: Read the last sentence — what is the queen bee's special job?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text says the queen bee lays all the eggs. You found the answer right in the words. Nice reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, let's reread. The passage tells one special job for the queen bee. Which job does it name just for her?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "Lays all the eggs." The text says that is the queen bee's special job.

questions.prompt:
Firefighters wear special gear to stay safe. Their helmets protect their heads. Their thick coats keep them safe from fire and heat.

Why do firefighters wear thick coats?
choices: To keep safe from fire and heat
questions.correct: To keep safe from fire and heat
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you exactly why!
questions.correct_feedback: Great reading! The text says thick coats keep firefighters safe from fire and heat. You found the reason in the words.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Reread the part about the thick coats. What does the passage say those coats protect firefighters from?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "To keep safe from fire and heat." The text says that is why firefighters wear thick coats.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: George Washington Carver was a scientist who loved studying peanuts. He worked hard to find hundreds of new ways to use them. From peanuts, he made useful things for people, such as soap and paint that families could use in their homes. Which of these items did George Washington Carver create using peanuts?
questions.hint: The passage lists examples of what Carver made from peanuts. Look for that list.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story says he made soap and paint from peanuts. You found the right one!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, go back to the story. It names the special things he made from peanuts. Which one did you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is soap. The story tells us he made soap and paint from peanuts for families to use.

questions.prompt:
Sea turtles travel thousands of miles through the ocean. They return to the same beach where they were born to lay their eggs.

Where do sea turtles lay their eggs?
choices: On the beach where they were born
In a nest in a tree
questions.correct: On the beach where they were born
questions.hint: Read the second sentence — which beach do they go back to?
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! They go back to the same beach where they were born to lay their eggs.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The story tells you the exact spot the turtles swim back to. Where were they born?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is the beach where they were born. The turtles travel far to return to that same beach.

questions.prompt:
In 1969, astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the Moon for the first time. Millions of people watched on television.

When did astronauts first walk on the Moon?
questions.hint: The year is right at the beginning of the passage.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It was 1969. The story gives us the exact year the astronauts walked on the Moon.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Look for the year written right in the story. Read it very carefully.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 1969. The story says that is the year Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the Moon.

questions.prompt:
Every morning, Mrs. Lee puts a dish of seeds on her back porch. Soon little birds fly down to eat. But when her cat sits by the door, no birds come at all. So Mrs. Lee keeps the cat inside during breakfast time.

Why does Mrs. Lee keep the cat inside in the morning?
choices: So the birds are not scared away
So the porch stays nice and clean
So the cat can sleep a little longer
So the cat will not eat the seeds
questions.correct: So the birds are not scared away
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells what happens when the cat is by the door. What does that clue tell you about why she moves the cat?
questions.correct_feedback: Nice thinking! When the cat sits by the door, no birds come. So she keeps it in to help the birds.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Think about what happens to the birds when the cat is near. Why would that be a problem?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is so the birds are not scared away. When the cat is out, no birds come to eat the seeds.

questions.prompt:
Sam plants a bean seed and watches it grow. First, a tiny root grows down into the soil. Next, a green stem pushes up. After many days, small leaves open at the top of the stem. Last of all, a flower appears.

When do the leaves open?
choices: At the very end, after the flower
After the stem grows but before the flower
Right after the root grows down
questions.correct: After the stem grows but before the flower
questions.hint: Put the four things in order. Then find where the leaves fit compared to the stem and the flower.
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! First the stem grows up, then the leaves open, and the flower comes last. You got the order!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Read the steps in order. What grows up first, and what comes right after the leaves?
questions.reveal_feedback: The leaves open after the stem grows but before the flower. The order is root, stem, leaves, then flower.

questions.prompt:
Ants are very strong for their size. When one ant finds a big crumb, it cannot carry the crumb alone. So the ant runs back to the nest and taps other ants with its feelers. Then many ants come and carry the crumb home together.

Why does the ant tap the other ants with its feelers?
choices: To warn them that danger is near
To show them the way back home
To ask them to help carry the crumb
To say hello to its friends
questions.correct: To ask them to help carry the crumb
questions.hint: Look at what happens right before and right after the tapping. What problem is the ant trying to solve?
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! The crumb is too big for one ant, so it taps the others to come help carry it.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Remember, one ant cannot carry the big crumb alone. Why would it go get the other ants?
questions.reveal_feedback: The ant taps the others to ask for help carrying the crumb. It is too big for one ant to move alone.

questions.prompt:
Our class has three helpers each day. The line leader walks in front when the class moves. The door holder keeps the door open for everyone. The light monitor turns off the lights when the class leaves the room. Today, Maya is the very last one to do her job.

What is Maya's job today?
questions.hint: Which job happens LAST, when the class is already leaving the room?
questions.correct_feedback: Smart! Maya does her job last, and the light monitor turns off the lights when the class leaves.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Maya's job happens LAST. Which helper does their job right as the class leaves the room?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is light monitor. That helper turns off the lights when the class leaves, so it is done last.

questions.prompt:
Deep down in the sea, some fish make their own light. They live so far down that sunlight from above cannot reach them. Their glowing light helps them find food and see each other.

What can we tell about the deep sea from this text?
choices: It is dark because sunlight cannot reach it
It has many green plants growing
It is warm and full of sunshine
It is right near the top of the water
questions.correct: It is dark because sunlight cannot reach it
questions.hint: Why would a fish need to make its OWN light? Use that clue to picture what the deep sea is like.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The fish live so deep that sunlight can't reach them, so it must be dark down there.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about why the fish need to make their own light. What does that tell you about the deep sea?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is it is dark because sunlight cannot reach it. That is why the fish make their own light.

standards.standard_description: Identify the main topic of a multiparagraph text as well as the focus of specific paragraphs.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child what a passage is mostly about, then have them tell you what each paragraph focuses on.

questions.prompt:
Dogs are very special animals that make great pets. They are often called "man's best friend" because they are so loyal and friendly. Dogs love to play with their families and often follow them around. Having a dog can make your home feel happier and safer.

There are many different kinds of dogs, both small and big. No matter their size, most dogs love to run and play outside. They enjoy going for walks and playing fetch with a ball. It's important to give dogs exercise so they stay healthy and happy.

Some dogs even have important jobs! For example, guide dogs help people who cannot see to walk safely. Police dogs work with officers to keep communities safe. Other dogs can help find people who are lost. These working dogs show how smart and helpful dogs can be.

What is the main topic of the text?
choices: Dogs need to exercise and play often.
Why dogs make wonderful pets and helpers.
How dogs help people with important jobs.
The different sizes and kinds of dogs.
questions.correct: Why dogs make wonderful pets and helpers.
questions.hint: To find the main topic, think about what all the paragraphs in the text are mostly about together.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The whole text tells why dogs are wonderful pets AND helpers. That is the big main idea.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. That is just one part of the text. Think about what ALL the paragraphs are about together.
questions.reveal_feedback: The main topic is why dogs make wonderful pets and helpers. That big idea covers the whole text.

questions.prompt:
Paragraph 1: Apples come in many colors — red, green, and yellow. Paragraph 2: Apples are good for you because they have vitamins and fiber.

What is Paragraph 2 mostly about?
questions.hint: Paragraph 2 talks about vitamins and fiber. That means it's about health!
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! Paragraph 2 tells about vitamins and fiber, so it is all about why apples are healthy.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Read Paragraph 2 again. It talks about vitamins and fiber. What big idea is that about?
questions.reveal_feedback: Paragraph 2 is about why apples are healthy. It says apples have vitamins and fiber that are good for you.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage below.

**Penguins!**

Penguins are amazing birds that live in very cold places, mostly in the Southern Hemisphere. You can find many of them in Antarctica, where it is snowy and icy. They love to be near the ocean because that is where they find their food. Even though they are birds, penguins cannot fly! They are excellent swimmers.

What do penguins eat? These busy birds are very good hunters in the water. They dive deep to catch tasty fish, tiny shrimp called krill, and other small sea animals. Penguins use their strong flippers to "fly" through the water as they chase their meals.

When it's time for baby penguins, the parents work together. The mother penguin lays one or two eggs. Both mom and dad take turns keeping the eggs warm until the chicks hatch. Once the babies are born, their parents feed them and protect them from the cold. Soon, the little penguins are big enough to swim and find their own food!

What is the MAIN topic of the whole passage?
questions.hint: To find the main topic, think about what all the paragraphs teach you together.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The passage tells where penguins live, what they eat, and about their babies. It's all about penguins!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Each of those is only ONE part. Think about what the whole passage is about from start to end.
questions.reveal_feedback: The main topic is all about penguins. The passage tells many things, so the big idea covers all of it.

questions.prompt:
Water is all around us, and it can look very different! Sometimes it's hard like a rock, and sometimes it flows like a river. Water can even disappear into the air. This is because water can change its shape or form.

One form of water is a solid. When water gets very cold, it freezes and turns into ice. Ice is solid water. You can hold ice in your hand, and it keeps its shape. Ice cubes in your drink and snow on the ground are examples of solid water.

Another form of water is a liquid. When ice melts, it becomes liquid water. This is the water you drink, use to wash your hands, or see in a pond. Liquid water flows and takes the shape of its container. It's the most common form of water we see every day.

The third form of water is a gas. When liquid water gets very hot, it turns into a gas called steam or water vapor. You can sometimes see steam rising from a hot pot of water. Water vapor is in the air all around us, even if we can't always see it. It helps make clouds!

Based on the entire text, what is the main topic?
choices: The three ways water can be found
questions.correct: The three ways water can be found
questions.hint: To find the main topic, think about what all the paragraphs are mostly talking about together.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! The text tells about solid, liquid, and gas, so it is about the three ways water can be found.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. That is only a small part. The text tells about ice, water, and steam. What is that all about?
questions.reveal_feedback: The main topic is the three ways water can be found. Water can be a solid, a liquid, or a gas.

questions.prompt: Read the passage below. What is the main topic of the passage? Many people love to have pets. Pets are animals that live with us in our homes. They can be fun and bring us joy. Dogs are popular pets. They like to play fetch and go for walks. Dogs need food, water, and lots of love. Cats are also common pets. They like to nap in sunny spots and chase toys. Cats need food, water, and a cozy place to sleep. Other pets include fish, birds, and hamsters. Fish swim in tanks. Birds sing songs. Hamsters run on wheels. No matter what kind, all pets need good care.
choices: How to take care of a cat.
Different types of animals at the zoo.
The many kinds of pets and how to care for them.
Why dogs are the best pets.
questions.correct: The many kinds of pets and how to care for them.
questions.hint: Think about what all the paragraphs are mostly about, not just one part.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! The text tells about dogs, cats, fish, and birds and how to care for them all.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. That is only one part. The text lists many pets, not just one. What is the big idea?
questions.reveal_feedback: The main topic is the many kinds of pets and how to care for them. It talks about lots of pets, not just one.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.

Bees live together in a hive with one queen bee. Bees fly from flower to flower and use the sweet juice to make honey. When they land on flowers, they move yellow dust called pollen, and this helps new plants grow.

What is this text MOSTLY about?
choices: The important jobs that bees do
How bees help new plants grow
Where the queen bee lives in the hive
How bees turn flower juice into honey
questions.correct: The important jobs that bees do
questions.hint: One choice covers the WHOLE text. The others are each true, but only tell about one small part.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! Bees make honey, move pollen, and live in a hive. The text is about all the jobs bees do.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. That is just one thing bees do. The text tells about a few of them. What idea covers them all?
questions.reveal_feedback: The text is mostly about the important jobs that bees do, like making honey and helping plants grow.

questions.prompt:
Read the paragraph.

Penguins cannot fly the way other birds do. But in the water they are amazing. Their wings work like flippers to push them fast, and they can dive deep down to catch fish.

What is this paragraph MOSTLY about?
choices: Penguins cannot fly like other birds
The kinds of fish that penguins eat
questions.hint: The first sentence sets up a surprise. Read the whole paragraph to find what it spends the most time telling you.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The paragraph tells how penguins use their wings to swim fast and dive deep. Great swimmers!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, most of the paragraph is about penguins in the water. What big idea is it telling you about them?
questions.reveal_feedback: The paragraph is mostly about how penguins are great swimmers. Their wings work like flippers in the water.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.

Rain forests are hot, and it rains there almost every day. So many animals live in them, like tiny bright frogs and huge, long snakes. Rain forests also give people things we use, like fruit to eat and plants that become medicine.

What is this text MOSTLY about?
choices: Why it rains so much in rain forests
The medicines that come from rain forest plants
What rain forests are like and why they matter
The huge snakes that live in rain forests
questions.correct: What rain forests are like and why they matter
questions.hint: A cool, exciting detail is not the same as the main topic. Ask what the text is about from start to finish.
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! The text tells what rain forests are like and why they matter to animals and people.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. That is just one detail. The text tells lots of things about rain forests. What is the whole idea?
questions.reveal_feedback: The text is mostly about what rain forests are like and why they matter. It gives many facts about them.

questions.prompt:
Read the paragraph.

Long ago, people had no clocks. They looked up at the sun to know the time. When the sun was high in the sky, it was the middle of the day. When the sun went down, they knew it was time to sleep.

What is this paragraph MOSTLY about?
choices: Why people needed to build the first clocks
Why people feel sleepy at night
How the sun moves across the sky each day
How people long ago used the sun to tell time
questions.correct: How people long ago used the sun to tell time
questions.hint: Several choices mention something the paragraph touches. Find the one big idea that ties every sentence together.
questions.correct_feedback: Great thinking! The whole paragraph tells how people long ago looked at the sun to know the time.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. That is only a small part. The paragraph is about how people once knew the time. How did they?
questions.reveal_feedback: The paragraph is mostly about how people long ago used the sun to tell time before there were clocks.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.

A librarian's day begins early. She turns on the lights and unlocks the big doors. All morning and afternoon she helps kids find books they will love. Before the library closes, she reads a story out loud so everyone can listen together.

What is this text MOSTLY about?
choices: The different ways a librarian helps at the library
Why kids love to check out library books
How a librarian opens the library in the morning
How to find a good book on the shelf
questions.correct: The different ways a librarian helps at the library
questions.hint: Don't just grab the topic of the very first sentence. Notice what she does across the WHOLE day.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The librarian opens up, helps kids find books, and reads a story. So many ways she helps!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. That is just one thing she does. The text tells about her whole day. What is the big idea?
questions.reveal_feedback: The text is mostly about the different ways a librarian helps at the library all day long.

standards.standard_description: Describe the connection between a series of historical events, scientific ideas, or steps in technical procedures.
standards.parent_tip: Help your child see how events or ideas connect — ask 'What happened because of that?'

questions.prompt:
First, the caterpillar eats lots of leaves. Then, it makes a chrysalis. Finally, it comes out as a butterfly.

What happens AFTER the caterpillar makes a chrysalis?
questions.hint: Follow the steps: eat → chrysalis → what comes last?
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! First it eats, then makes a chrysalis, and after that it comes out as a butterfly.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Find the chrysalis step, then read what comes right AFTER it. What happens next?
questions.reveal_feedback: After the chrysalis, it becomes a butterfly. The steps are eat leaves, make a chrysalis, then a butterfly.

questions.prompt:
In 1903, the Wright Brothers flew the first airplane. Their flight lasted only 12 seconds. Today, planes can fly across the whole world.

How are the events connected?
choices: The Wright Brothers' invention led to modern airplanes
The Wright Brothers flew across the world
questions.correct: The Wright Brothers' invention led to modern airplanes
questions.hint: The first event (1903 flight) started something big. What came from it?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The Wright Brothers flew first, and that led to the big planes we have today. Great connection!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about which came first, the short flight or big planes. How did one lead to the other?
questions.reveal_feedback: The Wright Brothers' invention led to modern airplanes. Their first flight came before today's big planes.

questions.prompt: After you spread peanut butter on one slice, what is the main reason you put the other slice of bread on top?
choices: So the sandwich will have more crunch.
To keep the peanut butter from falling out.
To make the bread softer to chew.
Because it is the last thing you do.
questions.correct: To keep the peanut butter from falling out.
questions.hint: Think about what happens to the peanut butter after you spread it and before you put the other slice on top.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The top slice holds everything in so the peanut butter doesn't fall out. Good thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Think about what the top slice is for. What would happen if you did not add it?
questions.reveal_feedback: You add the top slice to keep the peanut butter from falling out. It holds everything inside the sandwich.

questions.prompt:
When bees visit flowers, pollen sticks to their legs. They carry the pollen to other flowers. This helps new plants grow.

How does pollen getting on bees connect to new plants growing?
choices: Bees carry pollen to other flowers, which helps plants grow
questions.correct: Bees carry pollen to other flowers, which helps plants grow
questions.hint: Follow the chain: pollen on legs → carried to flowers → what happens?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The bees move the pollen from flower to flower, and that helps new plants grow. Nice job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. Follow the pollen. It sticks to the bees, then goes where? How does that help plants?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is bees carry pollen to other flowers, which helps plants grow. The pollen sticks to their legs.

questions.prompt: The passage explains that the sun makes ocean water evaporate. What must happen to the water vapor *after* it rises for clouds to form?
choices: It must get much saltier to stick together.
It becomes colder and joins with other tiny pieces.
It needs to get hotter from the sun's strong rays.
It is pushed quickly across the sky by fast winds.
questions.correct: It becomes colder and joins with other tiny pieces.
questions.hint: Look for the part of the passage that describes what happens to the water vapor as it goes up high in the sky.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! The water vapor rises, gets colder, and joins together to make clouds. You followed the steps!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Think about what happens to the vapor as it goes up high. Does it get warmer or colder?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is it becomes colder and joins with other tiny pieces. That is how clouds form up in the sky.

questions.prompt:
Sea otters eat sea urchins. When there are fewer sea otters, the number of sea urchins grows. As a result, the sea urchins eat too much kelp, and the kelp forests disappear.

What causes the kelp forests to disappear?
choices: Too many sea urchins eating the kelp
questions.correct: Too many sea urchins eating the kelp
questions.hint: Follow the chain: fewer otters means more urchins. What do the urchins do?
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! With fewer otters, the sea urchins grow, and they eat too much kelp. That's the cause!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. What eats the kelp? Follow the chain from fewer otters to the missing kelp forests.
questions.reveal_feedback: The kelp disappears because too many sea urchins eat it. With fewer otters, the urchins grow and eat too much.

questions.prompt:
Clownfish live inside sea anemones. The anemone's stinging tentacles protect the clownfish from predators. In return, the clownfish cleans the anemone and brings it food.

How are the clownfish and anemone connected?
questions.hint: The anemone protects the clownfish, and the clownfish helps the anemone. That's a two-way connection!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The anemone protects the clownfish, and the clownfish cleans and feeds it. They help each other!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read what each one does for the other. The anemone protects, and the clownfish cleans. What is that?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is they help each other. The anemone keeps the clownfish safe, and the clownfish cleans and feeds it.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: To make sea salt, workers first fill shallow pools with salty ocean water. Then they wait for hot, sunny days. The warm sun heats the water until it slowly disappears into the air, and only the salt is left behind in the bottom of the pool. When making sea salt, why is it important that the sun heats the ocean water?
choices: To make the water easier for workers to collect.
So the salt can become bright and shiny.
It causes the water to evaporate, leaving the salt behind.
To keep the shallow pools full of water.
questions.correct: It causes the water to evaporate, leaving the salt behind.
questions.hint: Look at what happens to the water right after the sun heats it, and what is left behind.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The sun heats the water until it disappears into the air, and the salt is left behind. Smart!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Think about what the sun does to the water. What is left in the pool after the water is gone?
questions.reveal_feedback: The sun makes the water evaporate, leaving the salt behind. That is how workers gather the sea salt.

questions.prompt:
Plastic bags look like jellyfish in the water. Because of this, sea turtles sometimes eat the plastic by mistake. This can make them very sick.

Why do sea turtles eat plastic bags?
questions.hint: The passage says the bags LOOK like something turtles normally eat!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In the water, plastic bags look like jellyfish, so turtles eat them by mistake. Good reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Read what the bags look like in the water. Why would a turtle eat one by mistake?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is the bags look like jellyfish. Turtles eat them by mistake, and that can make them sick.

questions.prompt: Based on the passage, how does the shallow ocean near the shore help plants grow on the ocean floor?
choices: The shallow water helps small fish find the plants easily.
It lets sunlight reach the bottom of the ocean, where plants need it to grow.
The shallow water makes the plants grow big and strong.
It makes the water very cold for the plants.
questions.correct: It lets sunlight reach the bottom of the ocean, where plants need it to grow.
questions.hint: Look at what happens because the ocean near the shore is shallow, and then what that allows to happen for the plants.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Shallow water lets sunlight reach the bottom, and plants need that light to grow. You got it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Think about what plants need to grow. What can reach the bottom when the water is shallow?
questions.reveal_feedback: Shallow water lets sunlight reach the ocean floor, where plants need that light to grow.

questions.prompt:
A seed needs water to grow. When it rains, the ground gets wet, and the wet ground helps a seed sprout into a tiny plant. But when there is no rain for many weeks, the ground turns dry and hard, and the seeds just stay under the dirt.

Why do the seeds stay under the dirt when it does not rain for weeks?
choices: The dry ground has no water the seeds need
Birds carried the seeds away in the rain
The wet ground is too soft for them
The seeds are too old to grow anymore
questions.correct: The dry ground has no water the seeds need
questions.hint: Find the one thing the seeds need in the first sentence, then check what dry ground is missing.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice thinking! Seeds need water to grow, and dry ground has none, so the seeds just stay put.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. What does a seed need to sprout? What is missing when the ground is dry and hard?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is the dry ground has no water the seeds need. Without rain, the seeds cannot sprout and grow.

questions.prompt:
To make a paper boat, Mia first folds the paper in half. Next, she folds the corners down to make a pointed top. Then she folds up the bottom edges to make the two sides. Last, she gently pulls the two sides open so the boat can float.

Why must Mia pull the sides open LAST and not first?
choices: The paper is easier to hold when it is flat
The sides must be folded before they can be opened
Pulling too early would make the paper wet
The corners cannot be folded once the boat is open
questions.correct: The sides must be folded before they can be opened
questions.hint: Look at which earlier step actually makes the sides. Can you open something that isn't there yet?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You can't open the sides until they are folded first. That's why it must come last. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about the order. What has to happen to the sides before Mia can pull them open?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is the sides must be folded before they can be opened. So Mia opens them last, not first.

questions.prompt:
Long ago, people had to walk or ride horses to travel, and it took a long time. Then trains were built, and people could go much farther in less time. Later, cars were invented, so families could leave right from their own homes and travel whenever they wanted.

What did cars let people do that trains did NOT?
choices: Ride only along the train tracks
Travel before trains were even built
Leave from their own homes whenever they wanted
Travel slower than a walking horse
questions.correct: Leave from their own homes whenever they wanted
questions.hint: Compare the train sentence with the car sentence, and find the new thing only cars added.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! Trains stay on tracks, but cars let families leave from home whenever they want. Good compare!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Think about how cars are different from trains. What new thing could families do with a car?
questions.reveal_feedback: Cars let people leave from their own homes whenever they wanted. Trains could not do that, they used tracks.

questions.prompt:
Bees fly from flower to flower to drink sweet nectar. As a bee lands, yellow dust called pollen sticks to its fuzzy legs. When the bee flies to the next flower, some of that pollen rubs off. Flowers need pollen from other flowers before they can make new seeds.

Why are bees important to flowers?
choices: They dig the flowers' seeds down into the ground
They drink up all the nectar so the flowers stay dry
They eat the pollen off of every flower they land on
They carry pollen between flowers so the flowers can make seeds
questions.correct: They carry pollen between flowers so the flowers can make seeds
questions.hint: Follow the pollen: where does it stick, where does it go, and what do flowers need it for?
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! Bees carry pollen between flowers, and that lets the flowers make new seeds. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. Follow the pollen on the bee's legs. Where does it go, and why do flowers need it?
questions.reveal_feedback: Bees carry pollen between flowers so the flowers can make seeds. The pollen sticks to their fuzzy legs.

questions.prompt:
Sam planted a bean seed in a cup of dirt on Monday, and he watered it every single day. He did not see anything happen at first. Then on Friday, Sam noticed a tiny green stem poking up out of the dirt, so he kept watering it.

What most likely happened to the seed between Monday and Friday?
choices: It slowly soaked up water and began to sprout under the dirt
It grew tall enough to open up its first flowers
It turned back into a bean for Sam to eat
It stayed exactly the same until Sam added new dirt
questions.correct: It slowly soaked up water and began to sprout under the dirt
questions.hint: You see the start (a seed) and the end (a tiny stem). Think about what step must have happened in between, hidden in the dirt.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Sam watered it each day, so under the dirt the seed soaked up water and started to sprout.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Sam watered it every day but saw nothing at first. What was the seed doing under the dirt?
questions.reveal_feedback: The seed slowly soaked up water and began to sprout under the dirt, then the green stem popped up on Friday.

standards.standard_description: Determine the meaning of words and phrases in a text relevant to a grade 2 topic or subject area.
standards.parent_tip: When your child encounters a new word, help them use the rest of the sentence to figure out what it means.

questions.prompt:
Nocturnal animals are active at night. Owls, bats, and raccoons are nocturnal.

What does **nocturnal** mean?
questions.hint: The sentence right after 'nocturnal' tells you — active at what time?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text says nocturnal animals are active at night, like owls and bats. You found the meaning!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The sentence right there tells you when nocturnal animals are busy. Read it again.
questions.reveal_feedback: Nocturnal means active at night. The text tells us owls, bats, and raccoons are busy in the nighttime.

questions.prompt: Frogs often live near ponds and streams. These wet places are their habitats. What does the word **habitat** mean in this sentence?
choices: A type of food a frog eats
A natural home for an animal
A game a frog likes to play
A kind of weather frogs enjoy
questions.correct: A natural home for an animal
questions.hint: Look at where frogs live and what those places are called.
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! A habitat is a natural home, like the ponds and streams where frogs live. Great work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the sentence says wet places are the frogs' habitats. What word means the place an animal lives?
questions.reveal_feedback: A habitat is a natural home for an animal. For frogs, that is wet places like ponds and streams.

questions.prompt:
Mammals are warm-blooded animals that have fur or hair. Dogs, cats, and humans are all mammals.

Based on the text, what makes an animal a mammal?
choices: It is warm-blooded with fur or hair
questions.correct: It is warm-blooded with fur or hair
questions.hint: The first sentence describes exactly what a mammal is.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text says mammals are warm-blooded and have fur or hair, like dogs and cats. Nice reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Read the sentence about mammals again. What two things does it say every mammal has?
questions.reveal_feedback: A mammal is warm-blooded with fur or hair. The text says dogs, cats, and humans are all mammals.

questions.prompt:
The ancient Egyptians built enormous pyramids. These huge stone structures still stand today.

What does **enormous** mean in this sentence?
questions.hint: The next sentence calls them 'huge stone structures.' That's a clue!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! Enormous means very huge. The pyramids are big stone structures, so enormous fits perfectly.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. The text calls the pyramids huge stone structures. What word means the same as very big?
questions.reveal_feedback: Enormous means very huge. The text calls the pyramids huge stone structures that still stand today.

questions.prompt:
Plants need nutrients from the soil to grow. Nutrients are like food for plants — they give plants energy and help them stay healthy.

What are **nutrients**?
choices: Food-like substances that help plants grow
questions.correct: Food-like substances that help plants grow
questions.hint: The second sentence explains it — nutrients are like food for plants!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text says nutrients are like food that gives plants energy and helps them grow. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Read the sentence again. It says nutrients are LIKE food for plants. What does that tell you?
questions.reveal_feedback: Nutrients are food-like substances that help plants grow. They give plants energy and keep them healthy.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a book about deserts:

"The desert gets almost no rain. To survive, a cactus stores water deep inside its thick stem. On the hottest days, when other plants wilt and die, the cactus stays green."

What does the word **stores** mean here?
questions.hint: Read the whole paragraph. Why would a cactus hold water inside its stem in a place with almost no rain? Use the clues about surviving the hottest days.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! The cactus keeps water inside its stem to use later on hot days. That's what stores means!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The cactus puts water in its stem to use on hot days. What does stores mean here?
questions.reveal_feedback: Here, stores means keeps something to use later. The cactus saves water in its stem for the hottest days.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a science book:

"Bees are busy helpers in a garden. As a bee crawls into a flower to drink, yellow dust sticks to its legs. The bee carries this dust to the next flower. This is how bees pollinate the plants so new seeds can grow."

What does **pollinate** mean in this paragraph?
choices: Sting animals that come too close
Move flower dust so seeds can grow
Drink sweet juice from a flower
Build a home inside a flower
questions.correct: Move flower dust so seeds can grow
questions.hint: The word comes right after the bee carries dust from flower to flower. Match the meaning to what the bee is DOING, not just what it eats.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The bee moves the yellow dust to the next flower so new seeds can grow. That's pollinate!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read what the bee does with the yellow dust. Where does it carry it, and why does that matter?
questions.reveal_feedback: Pollinate means to move flower dust so seeds can grow. The bee carries the yellow dust to the next flower.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a report about the ocean:

"Deep in the sea it is pitch black. No sunlight reaches down that far. Yet some tiny fish make their own light. The glowing spots help them find each other in the dark."

What does **pitch black** mean here?
questions.hint: Look at the two sentences right after the phrase. What do they tell you about the light down there? Let those clues decide the meaning.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! Deep in the sea no sunlight reaches, so pitch black means completely dark. You got it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. The text says no sunlight reaches that deep. What would pitch black mean down there?
questions.reveal_feedback: Pitch black means completely dark. Deep in the sea, no sunlight can reach, so it is totally dark.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a book about weather:

"After days of rain, the little creek began to swell. The water climbed over its banks and spread across the road. Soon cars could not drive through."

What does the word **swell** mean in this sentence?
questions.hint: Don't guess from the word alone. Read what happens next to the water, then pick the meaning that fits that whole picture.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! After lots of rain the creek got bigger and rose over its banks. That's what swell means!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. The water climbed over its banks and onto the road. What does swell mean about the creek?
questions.reveal_feedback: Swell means to get bigger and rise up. After days of rain, the creek grew and spread over its banks.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a book about the human body:

"When you run fast, your heart pumps harder. You breathe quickly, and your skin gets warm. Then your body sweats. The little drops of water cool you down so you don't get too hot."

What does the word **sweats** mean here?
choices: Makes drops of water to cool the body
questions.correct: Makes drops of water to cool the body
questions.hint: The sentence right after the word explains what the drops do. Use that clue instead of the other things the body does when you run.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! When you get hot, your body makes little drops of water to cool you down. That's sweats!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. The text says the little drops of water cool you down. What does sweats mean here?
questions.reveal_feedback: Sweats means the body makes drops of water to cool itself. Those little drops help you not get too hot.

standards.standard_description: Know and use various text features to locate key facts or information efficiently.
standards.parent_tip: Point out bold words, headings, and captions in nonfiction books and explain how they help readers.

questions.prompt: You want to find the meaning of the word **ecosystem** in a science book. Where should you look?
questions.hint: A glossary is like a mini-dictionary at the back of a book!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The glossary is where a book lists words and what they mean. Great place to look!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. You want the MEANING of a word. Which part of a book tells you what words mean?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is the glossary. That is the part of a book that lists words and tells you what they mean.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Maya opened a nonfiction book about the ocean. At the top of each part, she saw big, bold words like "Ocean Animals" and "Ocean Plants." Before she even started reading, those words told her exactly what each part of the book would be about. What is the purpose of a heading in a nonfiction text?
choices: To tell you what that section is about
questions.correct: To tell you what that section is about
questions.hint: Headings are like little titles for each section.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! A heading is the bold title at the top of a part, and it tells what that part is about.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about those big bold words at the top of each part. What do they tell you before you read?
questions.reveal_feedback: A heading tells you what that section is about. The bold words at the top show what each part will cover.

questions.prompt: You want to know which page has information about dolphins. Which text feature should you use?
questions.hint: An index lists topics alphabetically with their page numbers!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The index is a list at the back that shows which page each topic is on, so it takes you right to dolphins. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about which text feature is a list at the back that tells you what page each topic is on. Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: The best one is the index. It's the list at the back of a book that shows which page each topic is on, so you can find dolphins fast.

questions.prompt: In a nonfiction book, some words are in bold print. Why?
choices: To make the page look pretty
Because the author likes those words
To show important or new vocabulary words
questions.correct: To show important or new vocabulary words
questions.hint: Bold words stand out because they're important for the reader to know!
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! Authors put words in bold to show they are important or brand-new words you should learn. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think about why an author would make a word stand out darker than the rest. Is it just for looks, or to help you? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: Bold words are there to show important or new vocabulary words. The author makes them dark so you notice the words you need to learn.

questions.prompt:
Here is the Table of Contents for a book about weather:

Chapter 1: What Is Weather? ... page 2
Chapter 2: Clouds and Rain ... page 8
Chapter 3: Storms ... page 14
Chapter 4: Weather Tools ... page 20

Maria wants to learn how a rain gauge measures how much water falls. Which chapter should she turn to first?
questions.hint: Think about WHAT a rain gauge is, not just the word 'rain.' Match the idea to the chapter, not the matching word.
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! A rain gauge is a weather tool, so Chapter 4: Weather Tools is exactly where Maria should look. Smart reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A rain gauge is a tool that measures rain. Which chapter title talks about tools? Take another look at the list.
questions.reveal_feedback: Maria should turn to Chapter 4: Weather Tools. A rain gauge is a weather tool, so that chapter is where she'll find it.

questions.prompt:
Here is part of the Index from a bug book:

ants ... 4, 9
bees ... 7, 12
spiders ... 3, 15, 22
webs ... 15

Sam wants ONE page that tells about spiders AND their webs together. Which page should he open?
questions.hint: Look at the page numbers listed for 'spiders' and the page numbers listed for 'webs.' Find the number that shows up in BOTH lists.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Page 15 is listed for BOTH spiders and webs, so that one page tells about both together. Super sleuthing!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Look at the page numbers next to spiders AND next to webs. Which SAME number shows up on both lines? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: Sam should open page 15. It's listed next to both spiders and webs, so that one page tells about them together.

questions.prompt: While reading, you see a word printed in dark, thick letters: **migrate**. You do not know what it means and want to find out as fast as you can. What is the BEST thing to do?
choices: Read the whole chapter over again from the start
Look at the pictures on the page and guess
Look up 'migrate' in the glossary, where bold words are explained
Look up 'migrate' in the index to see the page numbers it is on
questions.correct: Look up 'migrate' in the glossary, where bold words are explained
questions.hint: You want the MEANING of the word. Think about which text feature gives meanings, and remember what bold letters usually signal.
questions.correct_feedback: Perfect! The glossary explains what bold words mean, so it's the fastest way to learn 'migrate.' Great choice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! You want the MEANING of a bold word, fast. Which book part explains what bold words mean? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The best thing is to look up 'migrate' in the glossary, where bold words are explained. It gives the meaning quickly.

questions.prompt:
A page in a book has these headings:

- Where Sea Turtles Live
- What Sea Turtles Eat
- How Baby Sea Turtles Grow
- Why Sea Turtles Are in Danger

Max wants to find out how sea turtles catch the fish they hunt for food. Which section should he read?
choices: Why Sea Turtles Are in Danger
questions.hint: The heading may not use the exact words in the question. Decide what BIG idea 'catching fish for food' belongs to, then match it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Catching and hunting fish is about food, so 'What Sea Turtles Eat' is the right section. Nice thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Max wants to know how turtles catch their FOOD. Which heading is all about eating? Take another look at the list.
questions.reveal_feedback: Max should read 'What Sea Turtles Eat.' Catching fish to hunt is about food, so that section is the right match.

questions.prompt: A book about planets has BOTH a table of contents in the front and an index in the back. Rosa wants to find EVERY page that has the word 'Mars' on it. Which one will help her find all of those pages faster?
choices: The index, because it lists a word and every page it appears on
The glossary, because it tells what the word 'Mars' means
The table of contents, because it lists the chapters in order
The title page, because it shows the name of the book
questions.correct: The index, because it lists a word and every page it appears on
questions.hint: You want every single page where one exact word shows up. Think about which list is sorted by words, not by chapters.
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! The index lists a word and EVERY page it's on, so Rosa can find all the Mars pages fast. Smart reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Rosa wants EVERY page with one word on it. Which tool lists a word and all the pages it appears on? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The index will help Rosa most, because it lists a word and every page it appears on. That's how she finds all the Mars pages fast.

standards.standard_description: Identify the main purpose of a text, including what the author wants to answer, explain, or describe.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child 'Why did the author write this? To teach us something, to tell a story, or to convince us of something?'

questions.prompt:
Brush your teeth twice a day. Use toothpaste with fluoride. Visit the dentist every six months.

What is the author's purpose?
choices: To explain how to take care of your teeth
questions.correct: To explain how to take care of your teeth
questions.hint: The author is giving you instructions about dental health.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author gives steps like brushing and visiting the dentist, so the purpose is to explain how to care for your teeth. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read the sentences again. They tell you what to do for your teeth. What is the author trying to help you do? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author's purpose is to explain how to take care of your teeth. Each sentence gives a tip, like brushing and seeing the dentist.

questions.prompt:
The blue whale is the largest animal ever to live on Earth. It can be over 100 feet long and weigh as much as 200 tons.

The author wrote this to —
choices: Tell a story about a whale
Convince you to visit the ocean
questions.hint: Is the author giving you facts or telling a story?
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! The author shares real facts about size and weight, so the purpose is to inform you about blue whales. Great work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The sentences give facts and numbers, not jokes or a story. What does the author want you to LEARN? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author wrote this to inform you about blue whales. It gives real facts, like how long and heavy they are.

questions.prompt:
Everyone should recycle! When we recycle, we save trees and keep trash out of the ocean. Please recycle your cans, bottles, and paper.

The author's main purpose is to —
choices: Teach you how paper is made
questions.hint: Words like 'should' and 'please' are clues that the author wants you to DO something.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says 'Please recycle' and gives reasons to do it, so the purpose is to persuade you. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The author is asking you to DO something and telling you why. What is the author trying to talk you into? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author's main purpose is to persuade you to recycle. Words like 'Please recycle' show the author wants you to act.

questions.prompt: A book that teaches you about different types of clouds was written to —
questions.hint: If a book teaches you facts, the purpose is to inform!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! A book that teaches you facts about clouds is written to inform. Nice job spotting that!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a book that TEACHES you facts isn't trying to scare or make you laugh. What does an author do when they teach facts? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The book was written to inform. When an author teaches you facts, like about clouds, their purpose is to inform.

questions.prompt:
Butterflies have four stages in their life: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, and butterfly. Each stage is an important part of their life cycle.

What question is the author trying to answer?
choices: What are the stages of a butterfly's life?
questions.correct: What are the stages of a butterfly's life?
questions.hint: The passage lists stages. What question does that answer?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The whole text lists the stages egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, and butterfly, so it answers what those stages are. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Look at what the whole text is about. It names egg, caterpillar, and more. What question does that answer? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author is answering: What are the stages of a butterfly's life? The text lists each stage, so that's the question it answers.

questions.prompt:
Have you ever watched a spider fix its web? First, the spider makes one strong line between two branches. Then it adds more lines that spread out like the spokes of a wheel. Last, it spins a sticky circle to catch bugs. Now you know how a spider builds its web!

What is the author's MAIN purpose?
choices: To explain the steps a spider takes to build a web
To convince you that spiders are helpful
To describe what a spider looks like
To tell an exciting story about a spider and a bug
questions.correct: To explain the steps a spider takes to build a web
questions.hint: Look at how the text is built: first, then, last. What are those words showing you?
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! The author uses First, Then, and Last to walk through building a web, so the purpose is to explain those steps. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Notice the order words First, Then, and Last. What is the author walking you through step by step? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author's main purpose is to explain the steps a spider takes to build a web. The words First, Then, and Last show the steps.

questions.prompt:
Why do leaves change color in the fall? In summer, leaves are full of green stuff that helps the tree make food. When the days get short and cold, that green stuff fades away. Then we can finally see the red and orange colors that were hiding all along.

The author wrote this text mostly to —
choices: Tell a story about a walk on a fall day
Answer why leaves change color in the fall
Explain how a tree makes its food in summer
Describe how pretty the red and orange leaves are
questions.correct: Answer why leaves change color in the fall
questions.hint: Two of the choices are true details from the text. Which one is the big question the whole text is answering?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text starts with a question and then gives the answer, so it was written to explain why leaves change color. Smart!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The text opens with a question and then answers it. What question is the author mostly answering? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author wrote this mostly to answer why leaves change color in the fall. It asks that question, then explains the reason.

questions.prompt:
Recess is the best part of the school day! Kids need time to run and play so their bodies stay strong. Playing outside also helps kids think better when they go back to class. Every school should give kids a long recess.

What is the author trying to do?
choices: Explain the rules of the games kids play at recess
Describe what a school playground looks like
Convince readers that schools should give kids a long recess
Inform readers about how the body stays strong
questions.correct: Convince readers that schools should give kids a long recess
questions.hint: Some sentences give facts, but notice words like 'best' and 'should.' What does the author want you to believe?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says every school SHOULD give a long recess and gives reasons, so the goal is to convince readers. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The author is telling schools what they SHOULD do and why. What is the author trying to talk readers into? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author is trying to convince readers that schools should give kids a long recess. Words like 'should' show they want to persuade you.

questions.prompt:
The rainforest is loud and green. Tall trees block the sun, so the ground below stays dark and wet. Bright birds call from the high branches, and frogs sing near the little streams. Everywhere you look, something is moving.

The author mainly wants to —
choices: Convince readers to visit the rainforest
Explain why the trees grow so tall
Teach readers how to take care of frogs
Describe what the rainforest is like
questions.correct: Describe what the rainforest is like
questions.hint: Is the author giving you reasons WHY things happen, or painting a picture so you can imagine the place?
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! The author paints a word-picture of sights and sounds, so the purpose is to describe what the rainforest is like. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The author fills the text with sights and sounds, like birds and frogs. What is the author helping you picture? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author mainly wants to describe what the rainforest is like. The words paint a picture of its sights and sounds.

questions.prompt:
Some people think bats are birds because they fly. But bats are not birds at all — they are mammals, just like dogs and people. Bats have soft fur instead of feathers, and mother bats feed their babies milk.

Why did the author write this?
choices: To correct the idea that bats are birds
To describe what baby bats look like
To explain how dogs and people are alike
To tell readers where bats like to sleep
questions.correct: To correct the idea that bats are birds
questions.hint: The first sentence tells you a wrong idea some people have. What does the rest of the text do with that wrong idea?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author fixes a wrong idea by explaining bats are mammals, not birds. That's why they wrote it. Smart reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Some people believe something wrong about bats. What is the author trying to fix or set straight? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author wrote this to correct the idea that bats are birds. The text explains bats are really mammals, not birds.

standards.standard_description: Explain how specific images contribute to and clarify a text.
standards.parent_tip: When you see diagrams or photos in a book, ask your child what new information the picture adds.

questions.prompt: A science book has a paragraph about the parts of a plant. Next to it is a diagram with labels pointing to the roots, stem, leaves, and flower. How does the diagram help?
choices: It makes the page look nice
It shows you exactly where each plant part is
questions.correct: It shows you exactly where each plant part is
questions.hint: The paragraph tells you ABOUT the parts. The diagram shows you WHERE they are.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The labels point to each part, so the diagram shows you exactly where the roots, stem, and leaves are. Great work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what those labels and arrows are doing. What does a labeled picture help you see about the plant? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The diagram shows you exactly where each plant part is. The labels point right to the roots, stem, leaves, and flower.

questions.prompt: A text about weather says: "A thermometer measures temperature." Below it is a picture of a thermometer showing 32°F. What does the picture add?
choices: It replaces the need to read
It shows what a thermometer looks like and how to read it
questions.correct: It shows what a thermometer looks like and how to read it
questions.hint: The words explain what it does. The picture shows what it LOOKS like and a reading.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The picture lets you see a thermometer AND read the number on it, so it adds to the words. Nice thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The picture doesn't replace reading, and it does add something. What can you SEE in the picture that the words alone can't show? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The picture shows what a thermometer looks like and how to read it. Seeing the 32 degrees helps you understand the words.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: In her book about animals around the world, the author added a large, colorful map. On the map, she placed small pictures of animals on top of the different countries where they are found. By looking at it, readers could see which animals lived in each part of the world. Why would an author include a map in a book about animals around the world?
choices: To show where different animals live
questions.correct: To show where different animals live
questions.hint: Maps show locations. If the book is about animals around the world, what would a map show?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Animal pictures sit on the countries where they live, so the map shows WHERE different animals live. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The map has animals placed on different countries. What does putting animals on places help you learn? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author added the map to show where different animals live. The animal pictures sit on the places where you'd find them.

questions.prompt: A book about healthy eating includes a picture of a food plate divided into sections: fruits, vegetables, grains, protein, and dairy. This image helps the reader —
choices: See how much of each food group to eat
Know what to buy at the store
questions.correct: See how much of each food group to eat
questions.hint: The plate shows sections of different sizes — what does that tell you?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The plate is split into food-group sections, so the picture shows how much of each group to eat. Smart!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The plate is divided into sections for each food group. What does seeing those sizes help you understand? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The image helps the reader see how much of each food group to eat. The plate's sections show the right amount of each.

questions.prompt: A book says: "A fox has a bushy tail." Next to the words is a photo of a fox. What does the photo help you do?
choices: See what a fox looks like
questions.correct: See what a fox looks like
questions.hint: The picture shows you the animal the words are talking about.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The photo lets you see what a fox looks like, right next to the words about it. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a photo isn't a song or a way to swim. What does looking at a picture of a fox help you do? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The photo helps you see what a fox looks like. Seeing the picture goes right along with the words about it.

questions.prompt: A page about bugs has a photo of a ladybug with an arrow pointing to its red wings. What does the arrow do?
questions.hint: An arrow points to the exact part it wants you to notice.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The arrow points straight to the ladybug's wings so you know exactly which part it means. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! An arrow is like a pointing finger. Look where it's aiming on the ladybug. What is it pointing to? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The arrow points to the ladybug's wings. It shows you exactly which part of the bug the page is talking about.

questions.prompt: A book says: "This is how you tie a shoe." Below the words are step-by-step pictures of hands tying a shoe. How do the pictures help?
choices: They show each step of tying a shoe
questions.correct: They show each step of tying a shoe
questions.hint: Pictures can show you how to do each step, one at a time.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The step-by-step pictures show each part of tying a shoe, so you can follow along. Great work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! These pictures aren't music or cars. They go one after another. What do step-by-step pictures show you how to do? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The pictures show each step of tying a shoe. Going one step at a time helps you follow how it's done.

questions.prompt: A page about the sun has a big yellow picture of the sun. The words say the sun is bright and yellow. What does the picture add?
choices: It shows the sun's color and shape
questions.correct: It shows the sun's color and shape
questions.hint: Look at what the picture lets you SEE that the words describe.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The big yellow picture shows the sun's color and shape, matching the words. Nice thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the picture isn't about rain or a cold night. Look at that big yellow circle. What does it show you about the sun? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The picture shows the sun's color and shape. The big yellow circle matches the words that say the sun is bright and yellow.

questions.prompt: A book about a bike has a photo of a bike with a label that says "wheel" pointing to a wheel. What does the label help you learn?
questions.hint: A label names the part it points to so you know what it is.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The label 'wheel' points to the wheel, so you learn which part that is. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A label is a word that names a part. This one points at the bike. What part is it telling you about? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The label helps you learn which part is the wheel. The word 'wheel' points right to it on the bike.

standards.standard_description: Describe how reasons support specific points the author makes in a text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child 'What reasons does the author give?' after reading a persuasive or explanatory text.

questions.prompt: The author says recess helps kids "focus better in class." How does this reason help you understand why "recess is important for kids"?
choices: It lets teachers have more time to plan lessons.
It tells us that kids will like school more if they can focus.
It shows that playing makes kids too tired to pay attention.
It means kids can do their schoolwork better when they return to class.
questions.correct: It means kids can do their schoolwork better when they return to class.
questions.hint: Look at the part of the passage that talks about kids focusing better in class to see why that makes recess important.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! If kids focus better, they can do their schoolwork better when they get back to class. That reason backs up why recess helps. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The reason is about the KIDS and their focus in class, not the teachers or being tired. What does focusing help kids do? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The best answer: it means kids can do their schoolwork better when they return to class. Focusing better helps their learning.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: The author of the article wanted kids to drink more water every day. She explained that water helps your body in many important ways. Most of all, she said, drinking water helps your brain work better so you can think clearly and learn new things. Which of these is a reason the author gives for why you should drink more water?
choices: Water helps you grow very tall.
Water helps your brain work better.
questions.correct: Water helps your brain work better.
questions.hint: Look at the sentences that tell you *why* drinking water is good for your body.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says water helps your brain work better, and that's her reason to drink more. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about what the author actually SAID water does for YOU. It wasn't about plants or being sleepy. Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The reason the author gives is that water helps your brain work better. She said it helps you think clearly and learn.

questions.prompt:
Helmets should be worn when riding a bike. They protect your head if you fall. Studies show helmets reduce head injuries by 85%.

What supports the author's point that helmets are important?
questions.hint: The author uses a fact with a number to support the point. Which sentence has the number?
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The fact that helmets cut head injuries by 85% is strong proof they are important. Smart thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Colors and looking cool don't prove helmets keep you SAFE. Which sentence gives real proof that helmets protect you? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The support is that helmets reduce head injuries by 85%. That fact proves helmets really do keep you safer.

questions.prompt: Read the short text. "You should always wear a helmet when you ride your bike. Wearing a helmet is important because it keeps your head safe if you fall down. So, remember your helmet!" Which statement best shares the author's main point and the reason that supports it?
choices: The author's point is that bikes are fun, and the reason is they go fast.
The author's point is to wear a helmet, and the reason is it keeps your head safe if you fall.
The author's point is that helmets are colorful, and the reason is they are made of plastic.
The author's point is you might fall down, and the reason is you need to practice riding.
questions.correct: The author's point is to wear a helmet, and the reason is it keeps your head safe if you fall.
questions.hint: Reread the second sentence in the text to find what the author says is important and why.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The point is to wear a helmet, and the reason is it keeps your head safe if you fall. You matched them perfectly!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The text isn't about bikes being fun or colors. What does the author want you to DO, and WHY? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author's point is to wear a helmet, and the reason is it keeps your head safe if you fall. Those two go together.

questions.prompt:
Schools should have gardens. Gardens teach kids about science. They also help kids eat healthier because kids are more likely to eat vegetables they grew themselves.

Which reason explains HOW gardens help kids eat healthier?
choices: Kids eat vegetables they grew themselves
questions.correct: Kids eat vegetables they grew themselves
questions.hint: Look for the sentence with 'because' — that gives the detailed reason.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Kids eat more veggies when they grow them themselves, so that reason explains HOW gardens help kids eat healthier. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Being pretty or teaching science doesn't explain healthier EATING. Which reason connects gardens to eating veggies? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The reason is that kids eat vegetables they grew themselves. Growing their own food makes kids want to eat it, so they eat healthier.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Bats are helpful animals. One bat can eat over a thousand bugs in one night, so farmers have fewer pests on their crops. Some people are scared of bats. But bats almost never bother people, and they would rather fly away than come close." The author wants you to believe that bats are HELPFUL. Which sentence gives a reason for THAT point?
choices: One bat can eat over a thousand bugs in one night, so farmers have fewer pests.
Bats would rather fly away than come close.
Some people are scared of bats.
questions.correct: One bat can eat over a thousand bugs in one night, so farmers have fewer pests.
questions.hint: The passage makes two different points. Find the reason that matches the point about bats being helpful, not the point about bats being safe.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! One bat eating a thousand bugs so farmers have fewer pests shows exactly why bats are helpful. Sharp reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Some sentences are about people being scared, not about bats being HELPFUL. Which sentence shows a way bats HELP? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The reason is: one bat can eat over a thousand bugs in one night, so farmers have fewer pests. That shows bats are helpful.

questions.prompt: An author writes: "Kids should walk to school when they can. Walking gives your body exercise before the day even starts. It also means one less car on the road, so the air stays cleaner." Why does having one less car help keep the air cleaner?
choices: Because kids like to walk with their friends.
Because cars put dirty smoke into the air, so fewer cars means less smoke.
Because cars are faster than walking.
Because walking gives your body exercise.
questions.correct: Because cars put dirty smoke into the air, so fewer cars means less smoke.
questions.hint: The author never spells out WHY fewer cars help. Use the clue "one less car... so the air stays cleaner" to figure out what cars must be doing to the air.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Cars put dirty smoke in the air, so fewer cars means less smoke and cleaner air. Great connecting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think about what comes OUT of cars into the air. Why would fewer cars make the air cleaner? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The reason is that cars put dirty smoke into the air, so fewer cars means less smoke. That's how walking keeps the air cleaner.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Libraries are great places for kids. At the library, you can borrow any book for free, so you can read as many stories as you want. Libraries are usually very quiet. Some libraries were built a long, long time ago." Which sentence best supports the point that libraries are GREAT for kids?
choices: Some libraries were built a long, long time ago.
Libraries are great places for kids.
You can borrow any book for free, so you can read as many stories as you want.
questions.correct: You can borrow any book for free, so you can read as many stories as you want.
questions.hint: Every choice is true, but only one gives a REASON that shows why libraries are great for kids. Watch out for facts that are just interesting and for a choice that only repeats the point.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Borrowing any book for free so you can read tons of stories is a strong reason libraries are great for kids. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Being quiet or old doesn't really show libraries are GREAT for kids. Which sentence gives a real reason kids love them? Try again.
