questions.reveal_feedback: The best support is: you can borrow any book for free, so you can read as many stories as you want. That's why libraries are great for kids.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Read the label before you buy a snack. The label tells you how much sugar is inside. It also shows if there is something that might make you sick, like peanuts." Why does the author mention that the label shows peanuts?
choices: To tell you that all snacks are bad for you.
To show that peanuts taste good.
To show that labels are hard to read.
To give a reason that reading the label can keep you safe.
questions.correct: To give a reason that reading the label can keep you safe.
questions.hint: Ask what job the peanut detail is doing. How does knowing about peanuts help with the author's big point about reading the label?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Peanuts can make some kids sick, so pointing them out shows reading the label keeps you safe. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the author isn't saying snacks are bad or peanuts taste good. Why would knowing about peanuts matter for your safety? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author mentions peanuts to give a reason that reading the label can keep you safe. Some foods can make you sick, so the label warns you.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Dogs make good pets for families. A dog will play with children for hours. A dog can also learn to sit and stay when you teach it." The author wants to add ONE more reason that dogs make good pets. Which sentence should the author add?
choices: A dog will keep your family company so no one feels lonely.
A dog will bark loudly all through the night.
Dogs need to visit the vet once every year.
Dogs come in many different sizes and colors.
questions.correct: A dog will keep your family company so no one feels lonely.
questions.hint: A new reason has to make the author's point stronger, not weaker. Which sentence gives a real reason dogs are GOOD pets, instead of a plain fact or a downside?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Keeping your family company so no one feels lonely is another good reason dogs make great pets. Perfect choice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Barking all night or vet visits are not reasons dogs are GOOD pets. Which sentence gives one more GOOD reason? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The author should add: a dog will keep your family company so no one feels lonely. That's one more reason dogs make good pets.

standards.standard_description: Compare and contrast the most important points presented by two texts on the same topic.
standards.parent_tip: Read two short articles about the same topic and help your child find what each one focuses on differently.

questions.prompt: Text A says dogs are great pets because they are loyal. Text B says dogs are great pets because they can be trained. What do BOTH texts agree on?
questions.hint: What main point do both texts share?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! One text says loyal and the other says trainable, but BOTH agree dogs are great pets. Great comparing!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Loyal and trainable are what each text says on its OWN. What big idea do BOTH texts share? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: Both texts agree that dogs are great pets. They give different reasons, but they both share that same main idea.

questions.prompt: Read Text A and Text B about the ocean.Text A: The ocean is a big home for many amazing animals! Whales are the biggest animals in the ocean. They swim and sing loud songs. Small, colorful fish dart through coral reefs. Crabs walk sideways on the sandy bottom. Jellyfish float and glow. All these animals need a clean ocean to live and play.Text B: Our ocean needs help to stay clean! Sometimes, trash like plastic bottles and bags floats into the ocean. This trash can hurt ocean animals. Fish might try to eat plastic, or turtles can get tangled. We can help by putting our trash in a bin and recycling. Keeping the ocean clean helps all the animals stay safe and healthy.After reading both texts, what is the biggest difference between what Text A and Text B teach us?
choices: Both texts teach us about how big the ocean is.
Text A is about animals that live on land, and Text B is about ocean animals.
Text A describes different kinds of ocean animals, and Text B explains how to protect the ocean from trash.
Both texts tell us how to swim with whales.
questions.correct: Text A describes different kinds of ocean animals, and Text B explains how to protect the ocean from trash.
questions.hint: Think about the main topic for Text A (what it mostly talks about) and the main topic for Text B (what it mostly talks about). How are those main topics different?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Text A describes ocean animals and Text B tells how to keep the ocean clean, so that's the big difference. Sharp reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Both texts are about the ocean, not land or swimming. Think about what EACH text mostly teaches. How are they different? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The biggest difference: Text A describes different kinds of ocean animals, and Text B explains how to protect the ocean from trash.

questions.prompt:
Text A: "Bats use sound to find food in the dark." Text B: "Bats eat insects and help farmers by keeping pests away."

What does Text B tell you that Text A does not?
questions.hint: Read Text B — what information is new and not in Text A?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Only Text B mentions that bats help farmers by keeping pests away. Great job comparing the two!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Using sound was in Text A. Look at what's NEW in Text B only. What extra fact does it add? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: Text B tells you that bats help farmers, which Text A does not. Text B adds that bats eat insects and keep pests away.

questions.prompt: Read these two texts. Text 1: 'Dogs are playful pets. They have soft fur. Dogs love to run and fetch balls. They often make a barking sound. Dogs need to go outside for walks.' Text 2: 'Cats are quiet pets. They also have soft fur. Cats like to nap and play with string. They often make a meowing sound. Cats like to stay indoors.' Which statement is true about BOTH texts?
choices: Both pets need to go outside for walks.
Both pets make a barking sound.
Both pets like to play with string.
questions.hint: Look closely at what Text 1 and Text 2 say about how the animals feel.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Text 1 and Text 2 both say the pets have soft fur, so that's what they share. Nice comparing!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Walks and barking are only about one pet. Read both texts and find the fact that is TRUE for both. Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: Both texts say the pets have soft fur. That's the one fact that is true for both the dog and the cat.

questions.prompt:
Text A says: "Exercise keeps your heart healthy." Text B says: "Exercise helps you sleep better and makes your bones strong."

Which benefit is mentioned in Text A but NOT Text B?
questions.hint: Read Text A carefully — which benefit does it mention that Text B doesn't?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A healthy heart is only in Text A, while Text B talks about sleep and bones. Great job telling them apart!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Better sleep and stronger bones were in Text B. Which benefit shows up ONLY in Text A? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: A healthy heart is mentioned in Text A but not Text B. Text B talks about better sleep and strong bones instead.

questions.prompt:
Two short texts about bees.

Text A: Bees help flowers grow. When a bee lands on a flower to drink, yellow dust sticks to its legs. The bee carries that dust to the next flower. This helps the flower make seeds.

Text B: Bees are busy workers. A bee flies from flower to flower all day. It carries sweet food back to its hive to feed the baby bees.

Both texts tell you that a bee ____.
choices: moves from one flower to another
carries yellow dust on its legs
feeds the baby bees in the hive
questions.correct: moves from one flower to another
questions.hint: Cross out any idea that only ONE text mentions. Keep the idea both texts say in their own words.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Both texts show a bee going from one flower to another. That's the idea they share. Sharp reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Yellow dust, seeds, and baby bees each show up in only ONE text. What does a bee do in BOTH texts? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: Both texts tell you that a bee moves from one flower to another. That's the idea they both share.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about rain.

Text A: Rain is good for us. It fills the rivers we drink from and helps the crops on farms grow tall.

Text B: Too much rain can be a problem. When rain falls hard for many days, rivers can flood and cover the roads.

How are the two texts DIFFERENT?
choices: Text A says rain floods roads, but Text B says rain fills rivers
Text A says rain helps, but Text B says rain can cause trouble
Both texts say rain is dangerous
Text A is about rivers, and Text B is about the sun
questions.correct: Text A says rain helps, but Text B says rain can cause trouble
questions.hint: Ask what MAIN point each text is making about rain, then find the choice that matches each one correctly.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Text A says rain helps us, and Text B says too much rain can cause trouble. That's the difference. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Neither text says rain is all bad. Think about how each text FEELS about rain. How are they different? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: The difference: Text A says rain helps, but Text B says rain can cause trouble, like flooding roads.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about penguins.

Text A: Penguins have wings, but they cannot fly. They use their wings like paddles to swim fast and catch fish in the cold water.

Text B: Penguins stay warm in the freezing cold. Their thick feathers and a layer of fat keep the icy water away from their skin.

Both texts help you understand that penguins ____.
choices: have thick feathers to stay warm
paddle with their wings to swim
live where it is cold and near water
cannot use their wings to fly
questions.correct: live where it is cold and near water
questions.hint: Look for the idea you can find clues for in BOTH texts, even if neither says it in those exact words.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Text A shows penguins swimming in cold water and Text B shows them staying warm, so both tell us they live where it's cold and near water. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Feathers and paddling wings each come from only ONE text. What idea do BOTH texts help you understand? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: Both texts help you understand that penguins live where it is cold and near water. One shows them swimming, the other shows them staying warm.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about a class garden.

Text A: Our class grew tomatoes. We watered the plants every morning, and after many weeks the red tomatoes were ready to pick.

Text B: Our class grew sunflowers. We gave them water each day, and the tall yellow flowers took the whole spring to bloom.

What is ONE way both classes' plants were the same?
choices: Both plants grew tall in one week
Both plants needed water often to grow
questions.correct: Both plants needed water often to grow
questions.hint: Find something that is true in Text A AND true in Text B. Two of the choices are only true for one text.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Both classes watered their plants, so both plants needed water often to grow. Great comparing!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! One class grew red tomatoes and the other grew yellow sunflowers, so those aren't the same. What did BOTH plants need? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: Both plants needed water often to grow. Each class watered their plant every day, so that's the way they were the same.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about lunch at school.

Text A: Maya thinks packing lunch is best. You get to choose your own food, and you never have to wait in a long line.

Text B: Sam thinks buying lunch is best. The food is warm, and you do not have to carry a heavy bag to school.

What do Maya and Sam DISAGREE about?
choices: Whether packing or buying lunch is the better choice
Whether school food is warm enough
Whether lunch lines are too long
Whether lunch bags are too heavy
questions.correct: Whether packing or buying lunch is the better choice
questions.hint: A disagreement is the BIG question both writers answer differently, not just one small reason.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Maya likes packing and Sam likes buying, so they don't agree on which lunch way is better. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, both kids only talk about lunch. What is the big thing each one says is best? Read their first sentences again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is whether packing or buying lunch is the better choice. Maya says packing, Sam says buying. That's what they disagree about.

standards.standard_description: By end of year, read and comprehend informational texts in the grades 2-3 text complexity band proficiently.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage your child to read nonfiction about topics they enjoy to build reading stamina.

questions.prompt: Based on the text, why would an octopus change its color?
choices: To show how happy it is.
questions.hint: Reread the sentence that explains what octopuses do when they change color.
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! An octopus changes color to blend in so other animals can't spot it. That keeps it safe!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think about how an octopus stays safe. Would changing color help it stay out of sight? Look again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is to hide from other animals. When an octopus changes color, it blends in so animals can't see it.

questions.prompt:
Recycling one aluminum can saves enough energy to power a TV for three hours. Americans throw away enough cans each year to build more than 20 Eiffel Towers.

What is the author trying to show with these facts?
choices: The Eiffel Tower is made of cans
Recycling cans saves a lot of energy
questions.correct: Recycling cans saves a lot of energy
questions.hint: The author compares cans to TV power and Eiffel Towers. Why? To show recycling matters!
questions.correct_feedback: Nice work! One can saves TV energy, and we toss out a huge number of cans, so recycling saves lots of energy.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The facts are all about cans and energy. What big idea is the author showing? Read the facts once more.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is recycling cans saves a lot of energy. Each can saves TV power, so recycling all of them saves a lot.

questions.prompt:
Polar bears have black skin under their white fur. The white fur helps them hide in the snow. Their large paws work like snowshoes, helping them walk on ice.

What helps polar bears walk on ice?
questions.hint: Read the last sentence — what works like snowshoes?
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! The text says their large paws work like snowshoes to help them walk on ice. Nice reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Find the part about walking on ice. What does it say works like snowshoes? Look back at the text.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is their large paws. The text says the big paws work like snowshoes to help polar bears walk on ice.

questions.prompt: Over many years, what causes a volcano to slowly get taller and wider?
choices: The ground under the volcano stretches and pushes it up.
New layers of hardened lava build up on its sides.
Heavy rains wash dirt and rocks to pile around it.
Wind blows sand and dust onto the volcano's top.
questions.correct: New layers of hardened lava build up on its sides.
questions.hint: Look for the part of the passage that explains how volcanoes grow over time.
questions.correct_feedback: Wonderful! Lava hardens and stacks up layer by layer, so the volcano grows taller and wider over time.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Think about what comes out of a volcano and cools. What builds up on its sides over time? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is new layers of hardened lava build up on its sides. Each time lava cools and stacks, the volcano grows.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Beavers cut down trees with their strong front teeth. They drag the branches into a river and stack them until the water stops flowing. This makes a deep, calm pond behind the pile of wood. Beavers build their homes in the middle of these ponds. The deep water keeps hungry wolves and bears from reaching them.

Why do beavers stack branches until the water stops flowing?
choices: To make deep water that keeps them safe
To help wolves and bears cross the river
To have extra branches to eat later
To clear the trees away from the river
questions.correct: To make deep water that keeps them safe
questions.hint: Follow the chain: stopping the water makes a pond, and the last sentence tells you what the deep water does.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Stacking branches stops the water, making a deep pond. Deep water keeps wolves and bears away. Smart thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Why would beavers want the water deep? Read the last sentence about what deep water does.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is to make deep water that keeps them safe. Deep water stops wolves and bears from reaching the beavers.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Some frogs freeze almost solid in winter. Their hearts stop beating and they do not breathe. To an animal that finds one, the frog seems dead. But sugar in the frog's body keeps the inside of its cells from turning hard. When spring warms the ground, the frog thaws out and hops away.

What is the sugar's most important job for the frog?
choices: It warms the frog up when spring comes
It protects the inside of the frog's cells from freezing hard
It keeps the frog's heart beating all winter
It makes the frog taste bad to other animals
questions.correct: It protects the inside of the frog's cells from freezing hard
questions.hint: Find the sentence that says what the sugar does, and notice the word 'But' before it.
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! The sugar stops the inside of the frog's cells from turning hard, so the frog can thaw and live.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read the sentence about sugar again. What part of the frog does the sugar keep from getting hard?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is it protects the inside of the frog's cells from freezing hard. That's how the frog survives the winter.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Long ago, sailors could not tell how far east or west they were. Many ships got lost and crashed on rocks. Then a clockmaker built a clock that kept perfect time even on a rocking ship. By comparing the clock's time to the sun, sailors finally knew their place on the sea. After that, far fewer ships were lost.

What happened because the clockmaker built his special clock?
choices: Sailors no longer needed to watch the sun
The sea became calmer for the ships
Fewer ships got lost and crashed
Ships could sail faster than before
questions.correct: Fewer ships got lost and crashed
questions.hint: Read to the very end. What changed for the ships after the clock was invented?
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! The clock helped sailors know their place, so far fewer ships got lost and crashed. Well read!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! What changed after the clock was built? Read the very last sentence again to find out.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is fewer ships got lost and crashed. The clock helped sailors know where they were on the sea.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

A cactus lives where rain almost never falls. Instead of flat leaves, it has sharp spines, so very little water leaks out into the dry air. Its thick stem swells up like a sponge to store water from one rare rain. Roots spread out just under the sand to soak up a shower fast, before the sun dries the ground.

Why does having spines instead of flat leaves help the cactus?
choices: It can grow taller in the sand
It can store more water in its roots
It can soak up rain more quickly
It loses less water into the dry air
questions.correct: It loses less water into the dry air
questions.hint: The sentence about spines has a clue word 'so' that tells you what the spines do.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! Spines are thin, so much less water leaks out into the dry air. That helps the cactus save water!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The cactus lives where it barely rains. Why would spines instead of leaves help it? Read that part again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is it loses less water into the dry air. Thin spines let out far less water than flat leaves would.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Honey bees dance to talk. When a bee finds flowers full of food, it flies home and dances in a circle. The other bees crowd close and feel the dance with their feelers. Then they fly straight out to find the same flowers. A bee that finds no food does not dance at all.

What can you tell about a hive where the bees are NOT dancing?
choices: The bees have not found flowers with food
The bees are dancing in a different way
The bees are too tired to fly
The bees have too much food to eat
questions.correct: The bees have not found flowers with food
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you when a bee will not dance. Use that rule backwards.
questions.correct_feedback: Great thinking! A bee dances only when it finds food. No dancing means no flowers found yet. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The text says when a bee does NOT dance, something is different. Read the last sentence again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is the bees have not found flowers with food. A bee that finds no food does not dance at all.

standards.standard_description: Know and apply grade-level phonics and word analysis skills in decoding words.
standards.parent_tip: Practice sounding out new words together — break them into smaller parts.

questions.prompt: Which word has a long vowel sound?
questions.hint: A long vowel says its own name. Which word has the 'ay' sound?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Cake" has a long a that says its own name, ay. The silent e makes the vowel long. Great ear!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a long vowel says its own name. Say each word slowly. Which one has a vowel that sounds like its letter name?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "cake." The a says its name, ay, because the silent e at the end makes it long.

questions.prompt: What is the root word in **playing**?
questions.hint: Remove the ending '-ing' — what word is left?
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! "Play" is the root word. When you take off the ending -ing, "play" is left all by itself. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A root word is the base part before the ending. Cover up the -ing. What word do you see left?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "play." Take the -ing off of playing and the root word play is what stays.

questions.prompt: If you are *not feeling happy*, which word best describes how you feel?
questions.hint: Remember that the letters 'un-' at the start of a word often mean 'not' or 'the opposite of'.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! The prefix un- means not, so unhappy means not happy. Great job spotting it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Un- means not. Which word means not happy? Say each choice and listen for the happy part.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "unhappy." The prefix un- means not, so unhappy means not feeling happy.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **pumpkin**?
questions.hint: Clap it out: pump-kin. How many claps?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Pump-kin has two beats. Clap it out and you feel two parts. Nice counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Clap the word as you say it: pump...kin. How many beats did you clap? Try it again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 2. When you clap pump-kin, you hear two beats, so it has two syllables.

questions.prompt: What sound does the 'au' make in the word 'because'?
questions.hint: Listen carefully to how you say the middle part of 'because'. Think about other words where 'au' or 'aw' make a similar sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Great ear! In "because," the au sounds like aw, just like in "saw." Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say "because" slowly and listen to the middle. Which everyday word has that same sound? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is the sound of aw in "saw." The au in because makes that same aw sound.

questions.prompt: Which word has a long i sound? **stick, strike, string, script**
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. A silent e at the end often makes the vowel say its own name.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Strike" has a long i that says its name, and the silent e makes it long. Sharp listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! A long i says its name, like in "bike." Say each word slowly. Which one has that long i sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "strike." The i says its name because of the silent e, giving it a long i sound.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME vowel sound as **bread**? **bead, meant, dream, bean**
questions.hint: The letters 'ea' can say two sounds. Listen for the short e sound you hear in bread.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! "Bread" and "meant" both have the short e sound, like in "bed." Great matching!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say "bread" and listen to its short e sound. Now say each word. Which one matches that sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "meant." It has the same short e sound as bread, even though the others sound like long e.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME vowel sound as **first**? **farm, nurse, form, fair**
questions.hint: Different letters can make the same r-controlled sound. Listen to the middle of 'first' and match it.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! "First" and "nurse" have the same er sound made by ir and ur. Great ear!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say "first" and hear the er sound in the middle. Which word has that same sound? Say them all again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "nurse." The ur in nurse makes the same er sound you hear in first.

questions.prompt: Which word starts with an open syllable that has a long vowel, like **tiger** (ti-ger)? **rabbit, robot, napkin, basket**
questions.hint: Clap the word into two parts. If the first part ends in a vowel, that vowel is usually long.
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! "Robot" splits ro-bot, and the o ends the first part, so it stays long, like tiger. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! An open syllable ends in a vowel that says its name. Which word starts like ti-ger with a long vowel?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "robot." It breaks ro-bot, and the o ends the first syllable, so it stays long like in tiger.

questions.prompt: What is the base (root) word in **unhappily**?
questions.hint: Peel off the beginning part and the ending part. What word is left in the middle?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Take off un- and -ily and you are left with "happy," the base word. Awesome work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! A base word has no prefix or ending. Peel off un- at the front and the ending. What's left?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "happy." Remove the prefix un- and the ending -ily, and the base word happy is what remains.

standards.standard_description: Distinguish long and short vowels when reading regularly spelled one-syllable words.
standards.parent_tip: Practice identifying short vowels (like 'a' in 'cat') vs long vowels (like 'a' in 'cake').

questions.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **a** sound?
questions.hint: Short 'a' sounds like the 'a' in 'cat.' Which word has that sound?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Map" has a short a sound, like the a in "cat." Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a short a sounds like the a in "apple." Say each word slowly. Which one has that quick a sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "map." It has a short a sound, just like the a in apple or cat.

questions.prompt: Which word has a LONG **o** sound?
questions.hint: Long 'o' says its own name, like 'oh.' Which word sounds like that?
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! "Home" has a long o that says its name, and the silent e makes it long. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A long o says its own name, like "oh." Say each word. Which one has an o that sounds like its letter?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "home." The o says its name because the silent e at the end makes it long.

questions.prompt: The word **bed** has a —
questions.hint: Say 'bed' out loud. Does the 'e' say its name or make a short sound?
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! "Bed" has a short e sound, like the e in "pen." Great ear!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say "bed" slowly and listen to the vowel. Is that e short and quick, or long? Look again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is short e. The word bed has a short e sound, just like the e in pen.

questions.prompt: Which word has a LONG **e** sound? Bread, Sweep, Deck, Kept
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Does the vowel say its own name (like the /ee/ in 'see'), or is it a quick short sound? Watch out — the letters 'ea' don't always make a long sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! "Sweep" has a long e that says its name, spelled with the ee team. Nice listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! A long e says its name, like "ee." Say each word slowly. Which one has that long e sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "sweep." The ee makes a long e sound that says its own name.

questions.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **i** sound? Child, Print, Find, Might
questions.hint: Try each word: does the 'i' say its name (/eye/) or the quick /ih/ sound? Some words hide a long i even without a silent e.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Print" has a short i sound, like the i in "sit." Sharp ear!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a short i sounds like the i in "pig." Say each word. The others have a long i. Which one is short?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "print." It has a short i sound like pig, while the others say a long i.

questions.prompt: Which word has a LONG **a** sound? Draft, Sprain, Blast, Grand
questions.hint: Ignore the beginning blend and listen only to the middle vowel. Does 'a' say its name /ay/, or the short /a/ as in 'cat'?
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! "Sprain" has a long a that says its name, made by the ai team. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! A long a says its name, like "ay." Say each word slowly. Which one has that long a sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "sprain." The ai team makes a long a sound that says its own name.

questions.prompt: Which word has a LONG **u** sound? Trust, Prune, Brush, Crunch
questions.hint: Listen to the middle vowel after the blend. Does 'u' say its name (/oo/ as in 'tune') or the short /uh/ sound? A silent e at the end can change it.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! "Prune" has a long u that says its name, and the silent e makes it long. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A long u says its name, like "oo" in "tune." Say each word. Which one has that long u sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "prune." The silent e makes the u long so it says its own name.

questions.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **o** sound? Coach, Frost, Float, Groan
questions.hint: Say each word and listen to the 'o'. Does it say its name /oh/, or the quick short /o/ as in 'dog'? The 'oa' team is a clue for one sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Great ear! "Frost" has a short o sound, like the o in "dog." Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A short o sounds like the o in "hot." Say each word. The others have long o. Which one is short?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "frost." It has a short o sound like hot, while the others say a long o.

standards.standard_description: Know spelling-sound correspondences for additional common vowel teams.
standards.parent_tip: Practice vowel teams like 'oa' in boat, 'ee' in tree, and 'ai' in rain.

questions.prompt: In the word **boat,** what sound does the vowel team **oa** make?
questions.hint: Say 'boat' — the 'oa' makes the 'oh' sound!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In "boat," the oa team makes a long o that says its name. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say "boat" slowly and listen to the vowel sound. Does the o say its own name, or is it short? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is long o. In boat, the oa team makes a long o that says its own name.

questions.prompt: Which word has the **ee** vowel team?
questions.hint: Look for two e's side by side!
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! "Tree" has the ee vowel team, and it makes a long e sound. Well spotted!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A vowel team is two vowels together. Look for the word with double e. Which one has ee in it?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "tree." It has the ee vowel team, while the others have just one e.

questions.prompt: Which word correctly completes the sentence and has the same vowel sound as **rain**? We went to the park to ______.
questions.hint: Listen carefully to the long 'a' sound in 'rain' and find the word that makes that same sound and fits the sentence.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! "Play" has the same long a sound as "rain," and it fits the sentence. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say "rain" and hear its long a sound. Which word has that same sound AND makes sense in the park?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "play." It has the same long a sound as rain and fits the sentence about the park.

questions.prompt: What sound does the 'oa' vowel team make in the word 'goat'?
questions.hint: Think about how you say the word 'goat' and focus on the sound the letters 'oa' make together.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In "goat," the oa team makes a long o that says its name. Great ear!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say "goat" slowly and listen to the oa. Does the o say its own name, or is it short? Look again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is the long o sound. In goat, the oa team makes a long o that says its own name.

questions.prompt: Which vowel team is in the word **loud**?
questions.hint: Look at the middle of 'loud' — which two vowels are together?
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! "Loud" has the ou vowel team in the middle. Great job finding it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look at the two vowels sitting together in this word. Which vowel team do you see in the middle? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is ou. The word loud has the ou vowel team right in the middle.

questions.prompt: The vowel team **ea** usually says long e, like in **beach**. But it can also say short e, like in **head**. Which word below has **ea** saying the SHORT e sound?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud. Listen for the one where 'ea' sounds like the 'e' in 'bed,' not the 'e' in 'see.'
questions.correct_feedback: Great! In "bread," the ea makes a short e sound, like "head." You caught the tricky one!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Three of these say long e. Say each word and find the one where ea sounds short, like "head."
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "bread." Its ea makes a short e sound like head, while the others say long e.

questions.prompt: The vowel team **oo** makes two sounds. In **moon** it is long, but in **book** it is short. Which word has the SAME **oo** sound as **book**?
questions.hint: Say 'book,' then say each choice. Find the one whose 'oo' sounds just like it, not like 'zoo.'
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Hook" has the same short oo sound as "book." Great matching!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say "book" and hear its short oo sound. The others sound like "moon." Which word matches book?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "hook." It has the same short oo sound as book, while the others sound long like moon.

questions.prompt: The vowel team **ie** can say long i, like in **pie**, or long e, like in **chief**. Which word has **ie** saying the long e sound?
questions.hint: Read each word. Which one rhymes with a long-e word like 'shield,' not with 'my'?
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! In "field," the ie team makes a long e sound, like "chief." Sharp ear!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say each word. Some ie words sound like long i, as in "pie." Which one sounds like long e instead?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "field." Its ie makes a long e sound like chief, while the others say long i.

questions.prompt: The word **rain** makes its long a sound with the vowel team **ai**. Which word makes the long a sound with a DIFFERENT vowel team?
questions.hint: Three of these spell long a the same way as 'rain.' Look for the one that uses a-y instead of a-i.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! "Tray" makes long a with the ay team, not ai like the others. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Three words use the ai team for long a. Look for the one that spells long a a different way. Which is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "tray." It makes long a with the ay team, while the others use ai.

questions.prompt: A vowel team is two vowels working together to make one sound, like **oa** or **ee**. Which word below has NO vowel team?
questions.hint: Look at the vowels in each word. Which word has only one single vowel with no partner next to it?
questions.correct_feedback: Great! "Camp" has just one vowel, so there is no vowel team. Nice spotting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! A vowel team is two vowels together. Look at each word. Which one has only a single vowel?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "camp." It has just one vowel, so there is no vowel team, unlike the others.

standards.standard_description: Decode regularly spelled two-syllable words with long vowels.
standards.parent_tip: Help your child break two-syllable words into parts and sound out each part.

questions.prompt: Break the word **cupcake** into two syllables:
questions.hint: This is a compound word made of two smaller words!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Cupcake" breaks into cup-cake, two small words you already know. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, this word is made of two little words put together. Where should you split it so each part is a real word?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is cup-cake. It breaks between the two little words cup and cake.

questions.prompt: Which two-syllable word has a long **i** sound?
questions.hint: Say each word. Which one has the 'eye' sound in it?
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! "Spider" has a long i, spi-der, where the i says its own name. Great ear!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say each two-part word slowly. Which one has a vowel that says its name, a long i like "eye"?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "spider." It breaks spi-der, and the i says its name, making a long i sound.

questions.prompt: Which choice shows how to correctly say the word **robot**?
questions.hint: Remember how to sound out two-syllable words that have a long vowel sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! We say ROH-bot, with a strong first part and a long o. Great job saying it right!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say "robot" out loud. Which part do you say louder, and does the o sound long? Pick the match.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is ROH-bot. We stress the first part and the o says its long name.

questions.prompt: Break **open** into syllables:
questions.hint: The 'o' is long and open — it gets its own syllable!
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! "Open" breaks o-pen, with the o all by itself saying its long name. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Say the word slowly and listen for the two beats. Where do you split it so the o stays long? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is o-pen. The o ends the first part all alone, so it says its long name.

questions.prompt:
Three of these two-syllable words have a long vowel sound. Which word does NOT?

music   human   tulip   muffin
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and split it in two. If the first syllable ends in a vowel (mu-sic), that vowel is usually long. Which one has a closed first syllable with a short sound?
questions.correct_feedback: Great! "Muffin" is the odd one out. Its u is short, while music, human, and tulip have long vowels. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Three words have a vowel that says its name. Which word has a short vowel instead?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "muffin." Its u is short, while music, human, and tulip all have long vowels.

questions.prompt:
Which two-syllable word has a long **o** sound?

robot   rocket   pocket   locket
questions.hint: Break each word after the first vowel or after the first consonant. If the first syllable ends in the vowel, the o stays long. Try ro-bot versus rock-et.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Robot" splits ro-bot, and the o says its long name. The others have a short o. Sharp ear!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say each word slowly. Three have a short o, like "hot." Which one has an o that says its own name?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "robot." It breaks ro-bot, and the o says its long name, while the others are short.

questions.prompt: Which shows the correct way to break **tiger** into two syllables so the **i** stays long?
questions.hint: When one consonant sits between two vowels, try splitting BEFORE the consonant first. That leaves the first vowel at the end of its syllable, so it says its long name.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! "Ti-ger" splits after the i, so the i ends the syllable and stays long. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! For the i to stay long, it should end the first part all by itself. Which split lets the i do that?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is ti-ger. Splitting after the i lets it end the syllable alone, so it stays long.

questions.prompt:
Which two-syllable word has a long **a** sound?

mistake   basket   napkin   rabbit
questions.hint: Read the second syllable of each word. Look for a silent e on the end that can jump back and make the a say its name.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You nailed it. The first part says its own name, so it has that long a sound you hear when you say the letter a.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say each word slowly and listen. Which one has a vowel that says its own name, like the a in cake?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is mistake. Say it slow: mis-TAKE. That 'take' part makes the long a sound, just like cake.

questions.prompt:
Which two-syllable word has a long **e** sound?

zebra   lemon   wagon   rabbit
questions.hint: Split each word into two parts. If the first syllable ends in the vowel (ze-bra), that vowel is long. Compare ze-bra with lem-on.
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! The first part says the letter e's own name, so it has that long e sound like in me.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say each word out loud. Which one has a vowel that says its own name, like the e in tree?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is zebra. Say it slow: ZE-bra. That first part makes the long e sound, just like tree.

standards.standard_description: Decode words with common prefixes and suffixes.
standards.parent_tip: Teach your child that 'un-' means 'not' and '-ful' means 'full of' to help decode new words.

questions.prompt: The suffix **-ful** in **hopeful** means —
questions.hint: Hope + ful = full of hope!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When you add -ful to hope, it means full of hope. You found exactly what -ful tells us.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about a bucket packed to the top. What might -ful be telling you about the word?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is full of. The suffix -ful means full of, so hopeful means full of hope.

questions.prompt: What does it mean if something is "helpful"?
questions.hint: The word "helpful" has the suffix "-ful." Think about what "-ful" means when you add it to a word like "help."
questions.correct_feedback: Nice work! Helpful means full of help. The ending -ful tells us it is packed with help.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The ending -ful is a clue. Think about what -ful means when you add it to a word.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is full of help. The ending -ful means full of, so helpful means full of help.

questions.prompt: Read these four words: uncle, under, until, unpack. In which word is un- a PREFIX that means "not" or "reverse"?
questions.hint: Cover the un- and see if a real base word is left. "pack" is a word, but "cle," "der," and "til" are not.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A prefix is a word part added to the front. Here it means to un-do the packing, so it means reverse.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. A prefix must sit in FRONT of a real base word. Which word has a base word left over when you take off un?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is unpack. Take off un and you still have pack, a real word. Un means to reverse the packing.

questions.prompt: The word grinned was made by adding -ed to a base word. Which is the base word?
questions.hint: When -ed was added, the last consonant was doubled. Take off -ed and one extra n to find the short base word.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! Grinned comes from grin plus -ed. We just doubled the n before adding the ending.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Take off the -ed ending and listen. What smaller word is left that means a big smile?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is grin. Grinned is grin with -ed added, and we doubled the n. Grin means a big smile.

questions.prompt: The word riding was made by adding -ing to a base word. Which is the base word?
questions.hint: An e was dropped before adding -ing. Add the e back to the base, not just the letters you see.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Riding comes from ride. We dropped the silent e before adding -ing.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Take off -ing and think. What base word means to sit on a bike and go? Remember it may have a silent e.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is ride. Riding is ride with -ing added, and we dropped the e. Ride means to go on a bike.

questions.prompt: The word cried was made by adding -ed to a base word. Which is the base word?
questions.hint: A y was changed to i before -ed was added. Change the i back to y to find the base word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Cried comes from cry. When a word ends in y, we change the y to i before adding -ed.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Take off -ed and think about the base word. What word means to shed tears? It ends in y.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is cry. Cried is cry with -ed added, and the y changed to i. Cry means to shed tears.

questions.prompt: Read these four words: red, reptile, ready, rewrite. In which word is re- a PREFIX that means "again"?
questions.hint: Cover the re- and check for a real base word that could happen "again." "write" works; "d," "ptile," and "ady" do not.
questions.correct_feedback: Perfect! A prefix goes in front. Here it means to write again, so re means again.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. A prefix sits in FRONT of a real base word. Which word has a base word left when you take off re?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is rewrite. Take off re and you still have write, a real word. Re means to do it again.

standards.standard_description: Identify words with inconsistent but common spelling-sound correspondences.
standards.parent_tip: Some words don't follow the rules — help your child learn tricky words like 'said' and 'have' by sight.

questions.prompt: If you were trying to sound out each word below, which one would be the trickiest to read because its letter sounds don't follow the usual phonics rules?
questions.hint: Look for the word where the letters don't make the sounds you expect them to make when you try to read it aloud.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! This word does not follow the usual rules. You just have to remember how it sounds.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, three of these sound out just fine. Which one has letters that do NOT match the sounds you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is said. It looks like it should rhyme with paid, but it sounds like sed. You just memorize it.

questions.prompt: The word **have** is tricky because —
choices: It ends with 'e' but the 'a' is short
The 'a' doesn't make the expected sound
questions.correct: It ends with 'e' but the 'a' is short
questions.hint: Usually a word ending in 'e' has a long vowel. But 'have' has a short 'a'!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A silent e usually makes the vowel long, but in have the a stays short. That is the tricky part.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say have out loud. Words ending in e usually have a long vowel. Is the a in have long or short?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: it ends with e but the a is short. Usually an ending e makes the vowel long, but have breaks that rule.

questions.prompt: Which word has a silent letter?
questions.hint: Say each word. In one of them, a letter is there but you don't hear it.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! There is a letter at the start you do not hear at all. That letter is silent.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say each word slowly. In which one do you skip right over a letter and not hear it?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is Know. The k is silent, so you only hear no. Cup, stop, and lamp say every letter.

questions.prompt: In the word **love,** the **o** makes the sound of —
questions.hint: Say 'love' and 'cup.' The vowel sounds the same!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In love the o does not say its own name. It sounds like the u in cup.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. Say love out loud and listen to the o. Does it sound like bone, or more like the vowel in cup?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is short u, like cup. In love the o surprises us and sounds like the u in cup, not a long o.

questions.prompt: Which word below has a part that sounds different from how you would usually expect those letters to sound?
questions.hint: Read each word aloud and listen carefully. Which one has a letter or letters that don't make the sound you usually expect them to make?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! This word's letters do not make their usual sounds. You just have to remember it.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Three of these sound exactly how they look. Which one has a part that sounds different from what you expect?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is said. It looks like it should rhyme with paid, but it sounds like sed. It breaks the rules.

questions.prompt: In three of these words the letters **ea** make the long-e sound you hear in *eat*. Which word is the tricky one, where **ea** makes a SHORT-e sound instead?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud and listen to the middle sound. Three match 'eat' — one sounds like 'bed'.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! In this word ea makes a short e sound, like in bed, not the long e in eat.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Three of these have ea saying long e like eat. Which one has ea saying a short e like bed?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is bread. In bread the ea makes a short e sound, like bed. Clean, dream, and leaf all say long e.

questions.prompt: The word **said** does not sound the way its letters look — it sounds like /sed/. Which word below **rhymes with said**?
questions.hint: Ignore how 'said' is spelled and listen to how it sounds when you talk. Which word ends with that same sound?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Said sounds like sed, and this word ends with that same sound. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Remember, said sounds like sed. Say each word and find the one that ends with that same ed sound.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is bed. Said sounds like sed, and bed rhymes with it. Raid, paid, and made all use a long a.

questions.prompt: In the word **knot**, the first letter is silent — you only hear /not/. Which other word begins with a SILENT letter?
questions.hint: Say each word. Three start with the /n/ sound you can hear right away — one hides a silent letter before the 'n'.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! This word starts with a letter you do not hear, just like the silent k in knot.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Say each word slowly. Which one starts with a letter you skip over and do not hear at all?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is knee. The k is silent, so you only hear nee. Nut, net, and nest all say the n at the start.

questions.prompt: Words that end in silent **e** usually make the vowel say its long name, like *bone* and *home*. Which word BREAKS that rule — it ends in **e** but the vowel is not long?
questions.hint: Say each word. Three make the 'o' say its name (long o). One sounds like 'kum' — that's the tricky one.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! This word ends in e but the vowel is not long. It breaks the silent e rule.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Three of these have long vowels like bone. Which one ends in e but the vowel is NOT long?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is come. It ends in e but sounds like cum, with a short vowel. Rope, cone, and stone follow the rule.

questions.prompt: The word **would** is tricky: the **l** is silent and it sounds like /wood/. Which word below **rhymes with would**?
questions.hint: Say 'would' out loud, then find the word that ends with the same sound — watch out for words that just look similar.
questions.correct_feedback: You nailed it! Would sounds like wood, and this word ends with that same sound. Nice ear!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Would sounds like wood. Say each word and find the one that ends with that same sound.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is could. Would sounds like wood, and could rhymes with it. Both hide a silent l.

standards.standard_description: Recognize and read grade-appropriate irregularly spelled words.
standards.parent_tip: Practice sight words like 'people,' 'again,' and 'friend' — these need to be memorized, not sounded out.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct spelling:
questions.hint: Remember: 'i' before 'e' in friend!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! This is the tricky one to remember. The i comes before the e, and it spells a pal.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! This word is tricky. Think about which letter comes first, the i or the e, when you spell it.
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct spelling is Friend. Remember, the i comes before the e. A friend is a fri-END you like.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct spelling of the word that means **one more time**:
questions.hint: This tricky word ends with '-ain' like 'rain.'
questions.correct_feedback: Great! You picked the right spelling. This word means one more time.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say the word out loud. It ends with an a-i-n sound. Which spelling has all the right letters?
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct spelling is Again. It ends in a-i-n. Again means one more time.

questions.prompt: Which word is an irregularly spelled word you need to memorize?
questions.hint: Three of these words can be sounded out. One is tricky and needs to be memorized.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! This is a word you memorize because its letters do not follow the usual sounds.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, three of these sound out easily. Which word has tricky letters you have to memorize?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is Because. Its letters do not sound the way you expect, so you learn it by heart. Help, stop, and jump follow the rules.

questions.prompt: One of these words is spelled correctly. It means "for the reason that," as in "I stayed inside ___ it was raining." Which spelling is correct?
questions.hint: This word breaks the rules. Picture the whole word the way you have seen it in books, and remember it ends with the little word 'cause.'
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! This is the correct spelling, and it means for the reason that.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. This word is tricky. Read each spelling slowly, letter by letter. Which one has all the right letters?
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct spelling is Because. Say it as be-cause. It means for the reason that.

questions.prompt: Three of these words follow the regular sounding-out rules. One is an irregular word you must know by heart because the letters do not make their usual sounds. Which one is the irregular word?
questions.hint: Try to sound out each word. The one where the 'ai' does NOT say its normal sound is the tricky one.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! This word's letters do not make their usual sounds, so you learn it by heart.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Three of these sound out just fine. Which one has letters that do NOT match the sounds you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is said. It looks like paid but sounds like sed, so you memorize it. Run, cat, and sit follow the rules.

questions.prompt: In one of these words the letters 'gh' make the /f/ sound (like in 'ph'). Which word is it?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud and listen for an /f/ sound at the end. In the others the 'gh' is silent.
questions.correct_feedback: Great listening! In this word the letters gh surprise us and make the /f/ sound.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say each word out loud. Which one has gh that makes an /f/ sound, like the ph in phone?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is laugh. Say it: laff. The gh makes an /f/ sound. In high and night the gh is silent.

questions.prompt: One of these words is spelled correctly. It means "as much as you need," as in "I have ___ money." Which spelling is correct?
questions.hint: This is an irregular word. It ends with 'gh' even though you hear an /f/ sound. Picture how it looks on the page.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! This is the correct spelling. It means as much as you need.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. This word is tricky. It ends with a gh that sounds like /f/. Which spelling has all the right letters?
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct spelling is Enough. Say it: e-nuff. The gh makes an /f/ sound. It means as much as you need.

questions.prompt: One of these words is spelled correctly. It means "was able to," as in "I ___ hear the bird." Which spelling is correct?
questions.hint: This word has a letter you do not hear. Remember it belongs to the 'could, would, should' family that all share the same tricky ending.
questions.correct_feedback: You nailed it! This is the right spelling, and it means was able to.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! This word has a silent l inside it. Say it: kood. Which spelling has all the right letters?
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct spelling is Could. It has a silent l and sounds like kood. It means was able to.

standards.standard_description: Read with sufficient accuracy and fluency to support comprehension.
standards.parent_tip: Listen to your child read aloud — encourage them to read smoothly and with expression.

questions.prompt: Imagine you are reading a fun story to your classmates. Which way of reading would help everyone understand the story best?
choices: Reading very fast, almost like a race.
Reading each word slowly, stopping often to figure it out.
Reading smoothly, like you are telling the story, and saying most words correctly.
Reading so quietly that only you can hear yourself.
questions.correct: Reading smoothly, like you are telling the story, and saying most words correctly.
questions.hint: Think about how good readers make a story easy for others to listen to and understand.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Reading smoothly, like you are telling a story, helps everyone follow along and understand.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Picture a reader who is easy to listen to. Would racing, stopping a lot, or whispering help your friends understand?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is reading smoothly, like you are telling the story, and saying most words right. That helps everyone understand best.

questions.prompt: When you see an exclamation mark (!) at the end of a sentence, you should read it with —
questions.hint: An exclamation mark means the sentence has strong emotion!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! An exclamation mark shows big feeling, so you read it with excitement or strong emotion.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! An exclamation mark is a strong-feeling mark. How does your voice sound when you are really excited?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is excitement or strong feeling. An exclamation mark tells you to read with a big, strong voice.

questions.prompt: If you don't understand a sentence after reading it, what should you do?
choices: Ask someone to read the whole book
questions.hint: Good readers go back and reread to make sure they understand.
questions.correct_feedback: Great choice! Reading it again carefully helps your brain catch what you missed the first time.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a good reader does not give up or skip. What could you do with that same sentence to understand it better?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is read it again more carefully. Going back and reading slowly helps you understand what you missed.

questions.prompt:
"Stop!" yelled the crossing guard.

How should you read the word **Stop**?
questions.hint: The crossing guard 'yelled' — so you should read it like a yell!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A crossing guard yelling Stop needs a loud, firm voice so everyone hears and stops.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The guard YELLED it to keep kids safe. What kind of voice matches yelling an important warning?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is a loud, firm voice. The guard yelled Stop to keep everyone safe, so it needs a strong, clear voice.

questions.prompt:
Which of these words has TWO syllables? Read each one carefully:

through    yellow    bright    scratched
questions.hint: Clap the word out loud. Count the beats where your chin drops, not the letters you see.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You can clap this word into two beats: yel-low. That means it has two syllables.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Clap as you say each word. Which one has TWO claps, meaning two beats?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is yellow. Clap it: yel-low, that is two beats. Through, bright, and scratched each have just one.

questions.prompt:
Which word RHYMES with the word 'said'? (Say 'said' out loud first: it sounds like /sed/.)

seed    red    sad    side
questions.hint: Listen to the ending sound, not the spelling. Say 'said' slowly and match the last part.
questions.correct_feedback: Great ear! Said sounds like sed, and this word ends with that same sound.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Remember, said sounds like sed. Say each word and find the one that ends with that ed sound.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is red. Said sounds like sed, and red rhymes with it. Seed, sad, and side have different vowel sounds.

questions.prompt:
Which word has the same long-i sound and silent-e pattern as 'stripe'?

slide    sniff    spill    sprint
questions.hint: Find the silent e on the end that makes the i say its name. Cover the blend and check the vowel.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! This word has a long i and a silent e at the end, just like stripe.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Stripe has a long i and a silent e. Which word says its i's name and ends in a silent e?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is slide. It has a long i and a silent e, just like stripe. Sniff, spill, and sprint have short i sounds.

questions.prompt:
Which word has the same vowel sound as 'boil'? (Say 'boil': it has the /oy/ sound in the middle.)

toy    book    boat    bowl
questions.hint: The /oy/ sound can be spelled 'oi' or 'oy'. Listen for the sound, not the letters.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Boil has the /oy/ sound, and this word has that same /oy/ sound too.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Boil has the /oy/ sound in the middle. Say each word and find the one with that same /oy/ sound.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is toy. It has the /oy/ sound just like boil. Book, boat, and bowl each have a different vowel sound.

questions.prompt:
Which word BEGINS with the same sound as 'gentle'? (Say 'gentle': it starts with a /j/ sound.)

giant    goat    great    gum
questions.hint: The letter g can say /g/ or /j/. Say each word and listen to the very first sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Gentle starts with a /j/ sound, and this word starts with that same soft /j/ sound.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Gentle starts with a /j/ sound. Which word begins with that soft /j/, not a hard g like goat?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is giant. It starts with a soft /j/ sound, just like gentle. Great, goat, and gum start with a hard g.

standards.standard_description: Read grade-level text with purpose and understanding.
standards.parent_tip: Before reading, set a purpose: 'Let's read to find out how butterflies are born.'

questions.prompt: What did the farmer do to help his garden grow before summer?
choices: He watered the seeds and planted them.
He made soup with his vegetables.
He waited for the snow to fall.
questions.correct: He watered the seeds and planted them.
questions.hint: Look at the first two sentences to find out what the farmer did to start his garden and keep it healthy.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! To help a garden grow, the farmer planted seeds and gave them water. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about what a garden needs to START growing. What would a farmer do at the very beginning?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is he watered the seeds and planted them. That is how he helped his garden grow before summer.

questions.prompt: Before you start reading, it helps to ask yourself —
choices: What do I want to learn from this?
questions.correct: What do I want to learn from this?
questions.hint: Setting a purpose helps you focus while reading.
questions.correct_feedback: Great thinking! Asking what you want to learn helps you pay attention and find important parts.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A smart reader sets a goal before reading. Which question helps you focus on learning from the book?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is what do I want to learn from this? Asking that before you read helps you focus on the important parts.

questions.prompt:
Dolphins are mammals, not fish. They breathe air and feed milk to their babies. Dolphins are very smart and can learn tricks.

What makes dolphins different from fish?
choices: They breathe air and feed milk to babies
questions.correct: They breathe air and feed milk to babies
questions.hint: The passage says dolphins are mammals, not fish. What makes them mammals?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The passage says dolphins breathe air and feed milk to babies. That makes them mammals, not fish.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Fish also swim, have tails, and live in the ocean. Look back for what makes dolphins SPECIAL and different.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is they breathe air and feed milk to babies. The passage says that is what makes dolphins different from fish.

questions.prompt:
Ben's favorite book is about space. He reads it every week because he wants to be an astronaut someday.

Why does Ben read the space book so often?
choices: He wants to be an astronaut
It's the only book he has
questions.correct: He wants to be an astronaut
questions.hint: The sentence after 'because' tells you the reason.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ben reads it every week because he dreams of being an astronaut someday. Nice reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The story tells you Ben's big dream. Read again to find WHY he loves that space book.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is he wants to be an astronaut. The story says that is why Ben reads his space book so often.

questions.prompt:
Earthworms help gardens by making tunnels in the soil. The tunnels let air and water reach plant roots.

How do earthworm tunnels help plants?
choices: They make the garden look nice
They let air and water reach the roots
questions.correct: They let air and water reach the roots
questions.hint: Read the second sentence — what do the tunnels let through?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The tunnels let air and water reach the roots, which helps the plants. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look back at what the tunnels DO for the soil. What can now reach the plant roots because of them?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is they let air and water reach the roots. The passage says that is how earthworm tunnels help plants.

questions.prompt:
Which word rhymes with the word great?

great
questions.hint: Say each word out loud and listen to the ending sound. Don't trust the spelling — 'ea' can trick you.
questions.correct_feedback: Great ear! Great sounds like grayt, and this word ends with that same long a sound.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, this one is tricky. Great sounds like grayt with a long a. Find the word that ends with that same ate sound.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is plate. Great sounds like grayt, and plate rhymes with it. Seat, treat, and meat use a long e sound.

questions.prompt:
In which word does the letter c make the /s/ sound (like in 'ice')?

cent   cave   cup   cot
questions.hint: Look at the letter right after the c. C is soft (/s/) before e, i, or y, and hard (/k/) before a, o, or u.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When c is followed by e, it often makes the soft /s/ sound, like in ice.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say each word and listen to the c. Which one makes an /s/ sound like in ice, not a hard /k/?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is cent. The c makes an /s/ sound, like in ice. In cave, cup, and cot the c makes a hard /k/ sound.

questions.prompt:
How many syllables are in the word important?

important
questions.hint: Clap once for each vowel sound you hear: im - por - tant. Count the beats, not the letters.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You can clap it: im-por-tant. That is three beats, so three syllables. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Clap the word as you say it slowly: im-por-tant. How many claps, or beats, do you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 3. Clap it out: im-por-tant. That is three beats, so the word has three syllables.

questions.prompt:
Which word has a silent letter (a letter you do NOT hear when you say it)?

knee   nest   note   need
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Does every letter make a sound? Watch the very first letter of each word.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! This word has a letter you do not hear at all. That letter is silent.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Say each word slowly. Which one has a letter you skip right over and never hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is knee. The k is silent, so you only hear nee. Nest, note, and need say every letter.

questions.prompt:
Which word has the same vowel sound as the word boy?

boy
questions.hint: Listen for the /oy/ sound in the middle. Remember that 'oi' and 'oy' can spell the very same sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Coin has that same oy sound you hear in boy. Your ears are working great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say boy out loud and listen. Now try each word and feel which one matches that sound.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is coin. Boy and coin both make the oy sound, like boy and coin. Say them together!

standards.standard_description: Read grade-level text orally with accuracy, appropriate rate, and expression.
standards.parent_tip: Model reading aloud with expression — change your voice for different characters and show excitement!

questions.prompt: How should you read this sentence? "I can't believe we won the game!"
questions.hint: The exclamation mark and 'I can't believe' show strong, happy feelings!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That sentence is happy and loud, so reading it with excitement and energy shows how they feel!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Look at the exclamation mark. How would you sound if YOU just won a big game?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best way is with excitement and energy. They just won, so your voice should sound happy and full of life!

questions.prompt: When you read a question aloud, your voice should —
questions.hint: Try saying 'Are you coming?' — notice how your voice rises at the end?
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! Your voice goes up at the end of a question, like when you ask a friend something.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Ask a question out loud right now. Listen to what your voice does at the very end.
questions.reveal_feedback: Your voice should go up at the end. Questions lift up at the end, like when you say, are you ready?

questions.prompt: Imagine you are reading a story out loud to your younger brother or sister. What is the best way to read so they can easily understand and enjoy the story?
choices: Read the words very quickly so you can finish the book fast.
Read each word super slowly, one by one, with long pauses.
Read quietly to yourself so no one else can hear.
Read at a smooth speed, not too fast and not too slow, making the story sound clear.
questions.correct: Read at a smooth speed, not too fast and not too slow, making the story sound clear.
questions.hint: Think about how fast you should read so that the person listening can understand every word and follow the story easily.
questions.correct_feedback: Perfect! Reading at a smooth speed makes the story easy to hear and fun to follow.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Think about listening to a story. Too fast or too slow makes it hard to enjoy, right?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best way is to read at a smooth speed, not too fast and not too slow. That makes the story clear and fun to hear.

questions.prompt: How should you read this line from a character who is scared? "W-what was that noise?"
questions.hint: The dashes in 'W-what' show stuttering — the character is scared!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A scared character sounds shaky and nervous. Your voice matches how they feel!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The character is scared. How does a scared voice sound, not a happy one?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is a shaky, nervous voice. The character is scared, so your voice should shake a little to show it.

questions.prompt: Someone asks you: "WHO ate the last apple?" You answer by reading aloud: "Sam ate the last apple." Which word should you stress (say the strongest) to answer the question?
questions.hint: Stress the word that gives the exact information the question is asking for.
questions.correct_feedback: Great thinking! The question asks WHO, and Sam is who did it, so you say Sam the strongest.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The question asks WHO ate it. Which word tells you the person who did it?
questions.reveal_feedback: You should stress Sam. The question asks WHO, and Sam is the answer, so that word gets said the strongest.

questions.prompt: Read this line the right way: "We won!" she whispered. How should you read the part "We won!"?
choices: Softly, in a whisper, but excited
questions.correct: Softly, in a whisper, but excited
questions.hint: The words right after the quote (the tag) tell you HOW the person is speaking, even when there is an exclamation mark.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! She whispered, so you read it soft, but she's happy, so keep it excited too!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The word whispered is a big clue. But she is also happy she won. How do you mix both?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is softly, in a whisper, but excited. She whispered it, so stay quiet, but let the happy feeling show.

questions.prompt: Where should you take a short pause when you read this sentence aloud? "When the bell rang, the class lined up."
questions.hint: A comma is a signal to take a quick breath or pause.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! The comma after rang tells you to take a short breath there before you keep reading.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look for the comma in the sentence. A comma is like a little stop sign for a short pause.
questions.reveal_feedback: You should pause after rang. There is a comma right there, and commas tell you to take a short pause.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence aloud: "You ate the whole thing?" It has no question word like "what" or "who," but it ends with a question mark. How should your voice sound at the end?
questions.hint: A question mark tells your voice to rise at the end, even when the sentence looks like a statement.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It ends with a question mark, so your voice goes up, like a question, even without a question word.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Look at the very last mark in the sentence. What kind of mark ends a question?
questions.reveal_feedback: Your voice should go up, like a question. The question mark at the end tells you to lift your voice up.

questions.prompt: You are reading a story aloud. It says: "The house was silent. Slowly, quietly, she opened the creaky door." How should you read this part?
choices: Slowly and quietly, to build suspense
questions.correct: Slowly and quietly, to build suspense
questions.hint: Let the mood words — silent, slowly, quietly — guide how fast and how loud you read.
questions.correct_feedback: Perfect! The house is silent, so reading slowly and quietly builds the scary, exciting feeling.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! The words silent, slowly, and quietly are clues. Should this part sound loud and silly?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is slowly and quietly, to build suspense. The silent house needs a soft, slow voice to feel spooky.

standards.standard_description: Use context to confirm or self-correct word recognition and understanding, rereading as necessary.
standards.parent_tip: When your child reads a word wrong and the sentence doesn't make sense, encourage them to try again.

questions.prompt: You read: "The dog buried a bone in the yard." But you accidentally read **bone** as "bun." What should you do?
choices: Go back and reread because 'bun' doesn't make sense
Ask someone to read for you
questions.correct: Go back and reread because 'bun' doesn't make sense
questions.hint: Does 'The dog buried a bun in the yard' make sense? If not, try again!
